will trigger your Reaction. Then, you choose the 
action you will take in response to that trigger, or 
you choose to move up to your Speed in response 
to it. Examples include “If the cultist steps on the 

trapdoor, I’ll pull the lever that opens it,” and “If the 
zombie steps next to me, I move away.”
 When the trigger occurs, you can either take your 
Reaction right after the trigger finishes or ignore 
the trigger.
 When you Ready a spell, you cast it as normal (expending any resources used to cast it) but hold its 
energy, which you release with your Reaction when 
the trigger occurs. To be readied, a spell must have 
a casting time of an action, and holding on to the 
spell’s magic requires Concentration, which you can 
maintain up to the start of your next turn. If your 
Concentration is broken, the spell dissipates without taking effect.

title: Search Action
summary: Search Action.
body: Search Action.
tableUse: Use when a creature spends combat time looking for something; choose Perception for sensing or Investigation for deduction.
dmImpact: Keeps 2014 procedure separate from 2024 wording.
srdSection: SRD 5.1 > Combat > Actions in Combat > Search
srdText: Search When you take the Search action, you devote your attention to finding something. Depending on the nature of your search, the GM might have you make a Wisdom (Perception) check or an Intelligence (Investigation) check.
dmSummary: Search Action.
full_text: Search When you take the Search action, you devote your attention to finding something. Depending on the nature of your search, the GM might have you make a Wisdom (Perception) check or an Intelligence (Investigation) check.
page_ref: SRD 5.1 > Combat > Actions in Combat > Search

title: Search and Study Actions
summary: Use Search for perceptive discovery and Study for recalling or analyzing information.
body: The 2024 action list separates finding things through senses from learning or reasoning about information. The DM chooses the relevant ability and skill.
tableUse: Use Search for hidden creatures, tracks, or clues; use Study for lore, tactics, mechanisms, or deductions.
commonCalls: Search often uses Wisdom skills.
Study often uses Intelligence skills.
changeSummary: 2024 splits the old Search space into Search and Study.
dmImpact: Players have clearer language for spending an action to perceive versus analyze.
srdText:
Search [Action] When you take the Search action, you make a Wisdom check to discern something that isn’t obvious. The Search table suggests which skills are applicable when you take this action, depending on what you’re trying to detect. Search Skill Thing to Detect Insight Creature’s state of mind Medicine Creature’s ailment or cause of death Perception Concealed creature or object Survival Tracks or food

Study [Action] When you take the Study action, you make an Intelligence check to study your memory, a book, a clue, or another source of knowledge and call to mind an important piece of information about it. The Areas of Knowledge table suggests which skills are applicable to various areas of knowledge. Areas of Knowledge Skill Areas Arcana Spells, magic items, eldritch symbols, magical traditions, planes of existence, and certain creatures (Aberrations, Constructs, Elementals, Fey, and Monstrosities) History Historic events and people, ancient civilizations, wars, and certain creatures (Giants and Humanoids) Investigation Traps, ciphers, riddles, and gadgetry Nature Terrain, flora, weather, and certain creatures (Beasts, Dragons, Oozes, and Plants) Religion Deities, religious hierarchies and rites, holy symbols, cults, and certain creatures (Celestials, Fiends, and Undead)
full_text:
Search [Action] When you take the Search action, you make a Wisdom check to discern something that isn’t obvious. The Search table suggests which skills are applicable when you take this action, depending on what you’re trying to detect. Search Skill Thing to Detect Insight Creature’s state of mind Medicine Creature’s ailment or cause of death Perception Concealed creature or object Survival Tracks or food

Study [Action] When you take the Study action, you make an Intelligence check to study your memory, a book, a clue, or another source of knowledge and call to mind an important piece of information about it. The Areas of Knowledge table suggests which skills are applicable to various areas of knowledge. Areas of Knowledge Skill Areas Arcana Spells, magic items, eldritch symbols, magical traditions, planes of existence, and certain creatures (Aberrations, Constructs, Elementals, Fey, and Monstrosities) History Historic events and people, ancient civilizations, wars, and certain creatures (Giants and Humanoids) Investigation Traps, ciphers, riddles, and gadgetry Nature Terrain, flora, weather, and certain creatures (Beasts, Dragons, Oozes, and Plants) Religion Deities, religious hierarchies and rites, holy symbols, cults, and certain creatures (Celestials, Fiends, and Undead)

title: Use an Object Action
summary: Use an Object Action.
body: Use an Object Action.
tableUse: Use for involved item or environmental interactions, especially when a simple free interaction is not enough.
changeSummary: 2024 reframes this as the Utilize action; 2014 uses Use an Object wording.
dmImpact: Keeps 2014 procedure separate from 2024 wording.
srdSection: SRD 5.1 > Combat > Actions in Combat > Use an Object
srdText: Use an Object You normally interact with an object while doing something else, such as when you draw a sword as part of an attack. When an object requires your action for its use, you take the Use an Object action. This action is also useful when you want to interact with more than one object on your turn.
dmSummary: Use an Object Action.
full_text: Use an Object You normally interact with an object while doing something else, such as when you draw a sword as part of an attack. When an object requires your action for its use, you take the Use an Object action. This action is also useful when you want to interact with more than one object on your turn.
page_ref: SRD 5.1 > Combat > Actions in Combat > Use an Object

title: Utilize Action
summary: Use the Utilize action when an object or feature requires your action to operate it.
body: The 2024 rules use clearer action labels for interacting with objects and non-attack features. Many item or environmental interactions specify when Utilize applies.
tableUse: Use when a creature operates a device, item, tool, or environmental feature as its main action.
commonCalls: Read the item or feature text for whether it requires Utilize or another action.
Simple incidental interactions may still be folded into movement or another action.
changeSummary: Use an Object is reframed under the 2024 Utilize action.
dmImpact: Cleaner action language helps adjudicate items and environmental controls.
srdText:
Utilize [Action]
You normally interact with an object while doing 
something else, such as when you draw a sword as 
part of the Attack action. When an object requires 
an action for its use, you take the Utilize action.
full_text:
Utilize [Action]
You normally interact with an object while doing 
something else, such as when you draw a sword as 
part of the Attack action. When an object requires 
an action for its use, you take the Utilize action.

title: Opportunity Attacks
summary: Use your reaction to make a melee attack when a creature you can see leaves your reach without avoiding the trigger.
body: Opportunity attacks happen just before the provoking creature leaves reach. Disengage, teleportation, or forced movement often prevents the trigger depending on the exact rule text.
tableUse: Use when a creature voluntarily moves away from melee pressure.
commonCalls: You need a reaction available.
Reach matters; leaving one enemy's reach can trigger even if staying near another.
srdText:
Opportunity Attacks
You can make an Opportunity Attack when a creature that you can see leaves your reach using its 
action, its Bonus Action, its Reaction, or one of its 
speeds. To make the Opportunity Attack, take a Reaction to make one melee attack with a weapon or 
an Unarmed Strike against the provoking creature. 
The attack occurs right before the creature leaves 
your reach. See also “Playing the Game” (“Combat”).
full_text:
Opportunity Attacks
You can make an Opportunity Attack when a creature that you can see leaves your reach using its 
action, its Bonus Action, its Reaction, or one of its 
speeds. To make the Opportunity Attack, take a Reaction to make one melee attack with a weapon or 
an Unarmed Strike against the provoking creature. 
The attack occurs right before the creature leaves 
your reach. See also “Playing the Game” (“Combat”).

title: Two-Weapon Fighting
summary: Two-Weapon Fighting.
body: Two-Weapon Fighting.
tableUse: Check that both weapons and the action sequence qualify before allowing the bonus-action attack.
changeSummary: 2014 uses a bonus-action off-hand attack; 2024 can interact differently with Light/Nick.
dmImpact: Keeps 2014 procedure separate from 2024 wording.
srdText: Two-Weapon Fighting When you take the Attack action and attack with a light melee weapon that you’re holding in one hand, you can use a bonus action to attack with a different light melee weapon that you’re holding in the other hand. You don’t add your ability modifier to the damage of the bonus attack, unless that modifier is negative. If either weapon has the thrown property, you can throw the weapon, instead of making a melee attack with it.
dmSummary: Two-Weapon Fighting.
full_text: Two-Weapon Fighting When you take the Attack action and attack with a light melee weapon that you’re holding in one hand, you can use a bonus action to attack with a different light melee weapon that you’re holding in the other hand. You don’t add your ability modifier to the damage of the bonus attack, unless that modifier is negative. If either weapon has the thrown property, you can throw the weapon, instead of making a melee attack with it.

title: Two-Weapon Fighting
summary: The 2024 version is tied to the Light weapon property and can interact with mastery features that change action economy.
body: When the Light property grants an extra attack, follow that property and any mastery or feat text. The Nick mastery can move the extra light-weapon attack into the Attack action once per turn.
tableUse: Check the exact weapon properties, mastery, fighting style, and feat text before spending the bonus action.
commonCalls: Nick is the big action-economy change for light-weapon builds.
The damage modifier depends on the current 2024 property/feature text.
changeSummary: The rule is reorganized around weapon properties and mastery rather than only a default bonus-action rule.
dmImpact: Dual-wielding can be more flexible and less bonus-action hungry for the right weapon setup.
srdSection: SRD 5.2 > Feats > Fighting Style Feats > Two-Weapon Fighting
srdText: Two-Weapon Fighting Fighting Style Feat (Prerequisite: Fighting Style Feature) When you make an extra attack as a result of using a weapon that has the Light property, you can add your ability modifier to the damage of that attack if you aren’t already adding it to the damage.
full_text: Two-Weapon Fighting Fighting Style Feat (Prerequisite: Fighting Style Feature) When you make an extra attack as a result of using a weapon that has the Light property, you can add your ability modifier to the damage of that attack if you aren’t already adding it to the damage.
page_ref: SRD 5.2 > Feats > Fighting Style Feats > Two-Weapon Fighting

title: Bonus Actions
summary: You can take a bonus action only when a rule grants one, and normally only once on your turn.
body: A bonus action is not a smaller action you always have available; it must be granted by a class feature, spell, item, condition, or other rule.
tableUse: Ask what rule grants the bonus action before allowing it.
commonCalls: You choose the timing on your turn unless the rule specifies timing.
You cannot trade an action for an extra bonus action by default.
srdText:
Bonus Action
A Bonus Action is a special action that you can take 
on the same turn that you take an action. You can’t 
take more than one Bonus Action on a turn, and you 
have a Bonus Action to take only if a rule explicitly 
says so. See also “Playing the Game” (“Actions”).
full_text:
Bonus Action
A Bonus Action is a special action that you can take 
on the same turn that you take an action. You can’t 
take more than one Bonus Action on a turn, and you 
have a Bonus Action to take only if a rule explicitly 
says so. See also “Playing the Game” (“Actions”).

title: Reactions
summary: A reaction is an instant response to a trigger; you normally get one between the start of your turns.
body: A reaction can occur on your turn or another creature's turn if its trigger happens. After using it, you cannot use another reaction until your next turn begins.
tableUse: Require the trigger to be met and the creature to have its reaction available.
commonCalls: Opportunity attacks use reactions.
Readied actions use reactions.
srdText:
Reaction
A Reaction is a special action taken in response to 
a trigger defined in the Reaction’s description. You 
can take a Reaction on another creature’s turn, and 
if you take it on your turn, you can do so even if you 
also take an action, a Bonus Action, or both. Once 
you take a Reaction, you can’t take another one 
until the start of your next turn. The Opportunity 
Attack is a Reaction available to all creatures. See 
also “Opportunity Attacks” and “Playing the Game” 
(“Actions”).
full_text:
Reaction
A Reaction is a special action taken in response to 
a trigger defined in the Reaction’s description. You 
can take a Reaction on another creature’s turn, and 
if you take it on your turn, you can do so even if you 
also take an action, a Bonus Action, or both. Once 
you take a Reaction, you can’t take another one 
until the start of your next turn. The Opportunity 
Attack is a Reaction available to all creatures. See 
also “Opportunity Attacks” and “Playing the Game” 
(“Actions”).

title: Blinded
summary: A blinded creature cannot see, fails sight-based checks, attacks poorly, and is easier to hit.
body: The creature automatically fails ability checks that require sight. Its attack rolls have disadvantage, and attack rolls against it have advantage.
tableUse: Use for darkness, eye injuries, magical blindness, or sensory denial.
commonCalls: Blindsight, tremorsense, or other senses may change practical impact.
srdText:
Blinded [Condition]
While you have the Blinded condition, you experience the following effects.
 Can’t See. You can’t see and automatically fail any 
ability check that requires sight.
 Attacks Affected. Attack rolls against you have 
Advantage, and your attack rolls have Disadvantage.
full_text:
Blinded [Condition]
While you have the Blinded condition, you experience the following effects.
 Can’t See. You can’t see and automatically fail any 
ability check that requires sight.
 Attacks Affected. Attack rolls against you have 
Advantage, and your attack rolls have Disadvantage.

title: Charmed
summary: A charmed creature cannot harm the charmer and the charmer has social leverage over it.
body: The charmed creature cannot attack the charmer or target the charmer with harmful abilities or magical effects. The charmer has advantage on social ability checks with it.
tableUse: Use to limit hostility without taking full control of the creature.
commonCalls: Charmed is not mind control unless the effect says so.
srdText:
Charmed [Condition]
While you have the Charmed condition, you experience the following effects.
 Can’t Harm the Charmer. You can’t attack the 
charmer or target the charmer with damaging abilities or magical effects.
 Social Advantage. The charmer has Advantage on 
any ability check to interact with you socially.
full_text:
Charmed [Condition]
While you have the Charmed condition, you experience the following effects.
 Can’t Harm the Charmer. You can’t attack the 
charmer or target the charmer with damaging abilities or magical effects.
 Social Advantage. The charmer has Advantage on 
any ability check to interact with you socially.

title: Deafened
summary: A deafened creature cannot hear and fails checks that require hearing.
body: The condition mainly affects perception, communication, and effects that require hearing.
tableUse: Use for thunderous effects, silence-like results, or damaged hearing.
commonCalls: Being deafened does not stop verbal components by itself.
srdText:
Deafened [Condition]
While you have the Deafened condition, you experience the following effect.
 Can’t Hear. You can’t hear and automatically fail 
any ability check that requires hearing.
full_text:
Deafened [Condition]
While you have the Deafened condition, you experience the following effect.
 Can’t Hear. You can’t hear and automatically fail 
any ability check that requires hearing.

title: Exhaustion
summary: Exhaustion.
body: Exhaustion.
tableUse: Track the exact level and apply all lower-level effects too; long rests remove only what the rule allows.
changeSummary: 2014 condition wording is stored separately from 2024 wording.
dmImpact: Keeps 2014 procedure separate from 2024 wording.
srdText: Exhaustion Some special abilities and environmental hazards, such as starvation and the long--term effects of freezing or scorching temperatures, can lead to a special condition called exhaustion. Exhaustion is measured in six levels. An effect can give a creature one or more levels of exhaustion, as specified in the effect’s description. Level Effect 1 Disadvantage on ability checks 2 Speed halved 3 Disadvantage on attack rolls and saving throws 4 Hit point maximum halved 5 Speed reduced to 0 6 Death If an already exhausted creature suffers another effect that causes exhaustion, its current level of exhaustion increases by the amount specified in the effect’s description. A creature suffers the effect of its current level of exhaustion as well as all lower levels. For example, a creature suffering level 2 exhaustion has its speed halved and has disadvantage on ability checks. An effect that removes exhaustion reduces its level as specified in the effect’s description, with all exhaustion effects ending if a creature’s exhaustion level is reduced below 1. Finishing a long rest reduces a creature’s exhaustion level by 1, provided that the creature has also ingested some food and drink.
dmSummary: Exhaustion.
full_text: Exhaustion Some special abilities and environmental hazards, such as starvation and the long--term effects of freezing or scorching temperatures, can lead to a special condition called exhaustion. Exhaustion is measured in six levels. An effect can give a creature one or more levels of exhaustion, as specified in the effect’s description. Level Effect 1 Disadvantage on ability checks 2 Speed halved 3 Disadvantage on attack rolls and saving throws 4 Hit point maximum halved 5 Speed reduced to 0 6 Death If an already exhausted creature suffers another effect that causes exhaustion, its current level of exhaustion increases by the amount specified in the effect’s description. A creature suffers the effect of its current level of exhaustion as well as all lower levels. For example, a creature suffering level 2 exhaustion has its speed halved and has disadvantage on ability checks. An effect that removes exhaustion reduces its level as specified in the effect’s description, with all exhaustion effects ending if a creature’s exhaustion level is reduced below 1. Finishing a long rest reduces a creature’s exhaustion level by 1, provided that the creature has also ingested some food and drink.

title: Exhaustion
summary: Each exhaustion level subtracts 2 from d20 tests and reduces Speed by 5 feet; level 6 is death in the 2024 Free Rules.
body: Exhaustion is cumulative. For each level, reduce d20 tests by 2 and Speed by 5 feet. Finishing a long rest removes one level.
tableUse: Use as a gradual pressure track for hazards, deprivation, suffocation, and powerful effects.
commonCalls: The public 2024 Free Rules list death at level 6.
The penalty affects d20 tests rather than separate check/attack/save steps.
changeSummary: 2024 uses a uniform per-level penalty instead of six different effects.
dmImpact: Easier to remember and less swingy at early levels, but every level affects most d20 rolls.
srdText:
Exhaustion [Condition]
While you have the Exhaustion condition, you 
experience the following effects.
 Exhaustion Levels. This condition is cumulative. 
Each time you receive it, you gain 1 Exhaustion 
level. You die if your Exhaustion level is 6.
 D20 Tests Affected. When you make a D20 Test, 
the roll is reduced by 2 times your Exhaustion level.
 Speed Reduced. Your Speed is reduced by a number of feet equal to 5 times your Exhaustion level.
 Removing Exhaustion Levels. Finishing a Long 
Rest removes 1 of your Exhaustion levels. When 
your Exhaustion level reaches 0, the condition ends.
full_text:
Exhaustion [Condition]
While you have the Exhaustion condition, you 
experience the following effects.
 Exhaustion Levels. This condition is cumulative. 
Each time you receive it, you gain 1 Exhaustion 
level. You die if your Exhaustion level is 6.
 D20 Tests Affected. When you make a D20 Test, 
the roll is reduced by 2 times your Exhaustion level.
 Speed Reduced. Your Speed is reduced by a number of feet equal to 5 times your Exhaustion level.
 Removing Exhaustion Levels. Finishing a Long 
Rest removes 1 of your Exhaustion levels. When 
your Exhaustion level reaches 0, the condition ends.

title: Frightened
summary: A frightened creature has trouble acting while the fear source is in sight and cannot willingly move closer to it.
body: The creature has disadvantage on ability checks and attack rolls while it can see the source of fear. It cannot willingly move closer to that source.
tableUse: Use to control movement and pressure attacks without paralyzing the creature.
commonCalls: Breaking line of sight can remove the disadvantage but not necessarily the condition.
srdText:
Frightened [Condition]
While you have the Frightened condition, you experience the following effects.
 Ability Checks and Attacks Affected. You have 
Disadvantage on ability checks and attack rolls 
while the source of fear is within line of sight.
 Can’t Approach. You can’t willingly move closer to 
the source of fear.
full_text:
Frightened [Condition]
While you have the Frightened condition, you experience the following effects.
 Ability Checks and Attacks Affected. You have 
Disadvantage on ability checks and attack rolls 
while the source of fear is within line of sight.
 Can’t Approach. You can’t willingly move closer to 
the source of fear.

title: Grappled
summary: A grappled creature has Speed 0 and cannot benefit from speed bonuses.
body: The grapple ends if the grappler is incapacitated or if an effect removes the grappled target from the grappler's reach. The exact escape procedure depends on the edition and feature.
tableUse: Use to pin a creature in place, set up hazards, or stop movement.
commonCalls: Grappled is not restrained unless another rule says so.
Forced movement can break the grapple if it separates reach.
srdText:
Grappled [Condition]
While you have the Grappled condition, you experience the following effects.
 Speed 0. Your Speed is 0 and can’t increase.
 Attacks Affected. You have Disadvantage on attack rolls against any target other than the grappler.
 Movable. The grappler can drag or carry you 
when it moves, but every foot of movement costs it 1 
extra foot unless you are Tiny or two or more sizes 
smaller than it.
full_text:
Grappled [Condition]
While you have the Grappled condition, you experience the following effects.
 Speed 0. Your Speed is 0 and can’t increase.
 Attacks Affected. You have Disadvantage on attack rolls against any target other than the grappler.
 Movable. The grappler can drag or carry you 
when it moves, but every foot of movement costs it 1 
extra foot unless you are Tiny or two or more sizes 
smaller than it.

title: Incapacitated
summary: An incapacitated creature cannot take actions or reactions; 2024 also calls out bonus actions and concentration more explicitly.
body: Use the condition as a broad shutdown state. In 2024 wording, it also explicitly blocks bonus actions, breaks concentration, prevents speech, and affects surprise initiative interactions.
tableUse: Use when a creature is stunned, paralyzed, unconscious, or otherwise unable to act.
commonCalls: Many other conditions include incapacitated.
Check edition wording for concentration and speech details.
srdText:
Incapacitated [Condition]
While you have the Incapacitated condition, you experience the following effects.
 Inactive. You can’t take any action, Bonus Action, 
or Reaction.
 No Concentration. Your Concentration is broken.
 Speechless. You can’t speak.
 Surprised. If you’re Incapacitated when you roll 
Initiative, you have Disadvantage on the roll.
full_text:
Incapacitated [Condition]
While you have the Incapacitated condition, you experience the following effects.
 Inactive. You can’t take any action, Bonus Action, 
or Reaction.
 No Concentration. Your Concentration is broken.
 Speechless. You can’t speak.
 Surprised. If you’re Incapacitated when you roll 
Initiative, you have Disadvantage on the roll.

title: Invisible
summary: An invisible creature cannot be seen normally, is harder to attack, and attacks with advantage unless a special sense counters it.
body: Attack rolls against an invisible creature generally have disadvantage, and the invisible creature's attack rolls generally have advantage. In 2024, hiding can grant the Invisible condition while hidden.
tableUse: Use special senses, tracks, noise, and search checks to adjudicate whether enemies know the creature's location.
commonCalls: Invisible is not the same as undetected.
Blindsight and similar senses can reduce or remove the benefit.
srdText:
Invisible [Condition]
While you have the Invisible condition, you experience the following effects.
 Surprise. If you’re Invisible when you roll Initiative, you have Advantage on the roll.
 Concealed. You aren’t affected by any effect that 
requires its target to be seen unless the effect’s creator can somehow see you. Any equipment you are 
wearing or carrying is also concealed.
 Attacks Affected. Attack rolls against you have 
Disadvantage, and your attack rolls have Advantage. If a creature can somehow see you, you don’t 
gain this benefit against that creature.
full_text:
Invisible [Condition]
While you have the Invisible condition, you experience the following effects.
 Surprise. If you’re Invisible when you roll Initiative, you have Advantage on the roll.
 Concealed. You aren’t affected by any effect that 
requires its target to be seen unless the effect’s creator can somehow see you. Any equipment you are 
wearing or carrying is also concealed.
 Attacks Affected. Attack rolls against you have 
Disadvantage, and your attack rolls have Advantage. If a creature can somehow see you, you don’t 
gain this benefit against that creature.

title: Paralyzed
summary: A paralyzed creature is incapacitated, cannot move, fails Strength and Dexterity saves, and is vulnerable to close critical hits.
body: Attack rolls against the creature have advantage. A hit from within 5 feet is a critical hit. The creature cannot move or speak under 2014 wording; 2024 includes the same practical shutdown through condition interactions.
tableUse: Use for hold-person style effects and high-danger lockdown.
commonCalls: Close melee hits are automatic critical hits if they hit.
The creature still occupies space and can be targeted.
srdText:
Paralyzed [Condition]
While you have the Paralyzed condition, you experience the following effects.
 Incapacitated. You have the Incapacitated 
condition.
 Speed 0. Your Speed is 0 and can’t increase.
 Saving Throws Affected. You automatically fail 
Strength and Dexterity saving throws.
 Attacks Affected. Attack rolls against you have 
Advantage.
 Automatic Critical Hits. Any attack roll that hits 
you is a Critical Hit if the attacker is within 5 feet of 
you.
full_text:
Paralyzed [Condition]
While you have the Paralyzed condition, you experience the following effects.
 Incapacitated. You have the Incapacitated 
condition.
 Speed 0. Your Speed is 0 and can’t increase.
 Saving Throws Affected. You automatically fail 
Strength and Dexterity saving throws.
 Attacks Affected. Attack rolls against you have 
Advantage.
 Automatic Critical Hits. Any attack roll that hits 
you is a Critical Hit if the attacker is within 5 feet of 
you.

title: Petrified
summary: A petrified creature becomes an inert solid substance, is incapacitated, cannot move, and is much harder but helpless.
body: The target and carried nonmagical gear transform into a solid substance. It is incapacitated, its speed is 0, attacks against it have advantage, it fails Strength and Dexterity saves, and it resists damage in 2024 wording.
tableUse: Use as a severe battlefield removal effect with rescue potential.
commonCalls: Check the exact spell or monster effect for saving throws and reversal.
srdText:
Petrified [Condition]
While you have the Petrified condition, you experience the following effects.
 Turned to Inanimate Substance. You are transformed, along with any nonmagical objects you are 
wearing and carrying, into a solid inanimate substance (usually stone). Your weight increases by a 
factor of ten, and you cease aging.
 Incapacitated. You have the Incapacitated 
condition.
 Speed 0. Your Speed is 0 and can’t increase.
 Attacks Affected. Attack rolls against you have 
Advantage.
 Saving Throws Affected. You automatically fail 
Strength and Dexterity saving throws.
 Resist Damage. You have Resistance to all 
damage.
 Poison Immunity. You have Immunity to the Poisoned condition.
full_text:
Petrified [Condition]
While you have the Petrified condition, you experience the following effects.
 Turned to Inanimate Substance. You are transformed, along with any nonmagical objects you are 
wearing and carrying, into a solid inanimate substance (usually stone). Your weight increases by a 
factor of ten, and you cease aging.
 Incapacitated. You have the Incapacitated 
condition.
 Speed 0. Your Speed is 0 and can’t increase.
 Attacks Affected. Attack rolls against you have 
Advantage.
 Saving Throws Affected. You automatically fail 
Strength and Dexterity saving throws.
 Resist Damage. You have Resistance to all 
damage.
 Poison Immunity. You have Immunity to the Poisoned condition.

title: Poisoned
summary: A poisoned creature has disadvantage on attack rolls and ability checks.
body: The condition represents sickness, toxin, venom, or magical corruption that makes action unreliable.
tableUse: Use as a simple debuff that pressures attackers and skill users.
commonCalls: Poison damage and the poisoned condition are separate.
Immunity to poison damage may or may not imply immunity to the condition depending on stat block wording.
srdText:
Poisoned [Condition]
While you have the Poisoned condition, you experience the following effect.
 Ability Checks and Attacks Affected. You have 
Disadvantage on attack rolls and ability checks.
full_text:
Poisoned [Condition]
While you have the Poisoned condition, you experience the following effect.
 Ability Checks and Attacks Affected. You have 
Disadvantage on attack rolls and ability checks.

title: Prone
summary: A prone creature crawls or spends movement to stand, attacks at disadvantage, and is easier to hit in melee.
body: The creature's only movement option is crawling unless it stands up. It has disadvantage on attack rolls. Attacks from within 5 feet have advantage; attacks from farther away have disadvantage.
tableUse: Use for trips, falls, shove effects, and low-position tactics.
commonCalls: Standing costs movement, not an action.
A flying creature knocked prone may fall unless it can hover or has a rule preventing the fall.
srdText:
Prone [Condition]
While you have the Prone condition, you experience 
the following effects.
 Restricted Movement. Your only movement options are to crawl or to spend an amount of movement equal to half your Speed (round down) to 
right yourself and thereby end the condition. If your 
Speed is 0, you can’t right yourself.
 Attacks Affected. You have Disadvantage on attack rolls. An attack roll against you has Advantage 
if the attacker is within 5 feet of you. Otherwise, 
that attack roll has Disadvantage.
full_text:
Prone [Condition]
While you have the Prone condition, you experience 
the following effects.
 Restricted Movement. Your only movement options are to crawl or to spend an amount of movement equal to half your Speed (round down) to 
right yourself and thereby end the condition. If your 
Speed is 0, you can’t right yourself.
 Attacks Affected. You have Disadvantage on attack rolls. An attack roll against you has Advantage 
if the attacker is within 5 feet of you. Otherwise, 
that attack roll has Disadvantage.

title: Restrained
summary: A restrained creature has Speed 0, attacks poorly, is easier to hit, and has disadvantage on Dexterity saves.
body: The creature cannot benefit from speed bonuses. Its attack rolls have disadvantage, attack rolls against it have advantage, and Dexterity saving throws have disadvantage.
tableUse: Use for webs, nets, vines, tentacles, and binding effects.
commonCalls: Restrained is stronger than grappled.
Forced movement may not end restrained unless the effect says so.
srdText:
Restrained [Condition]
While you have the Restrained condition, you experience the following effects.
 Speed 0. Your Speed is 0 and can’t increase.
 Attacks Affected. Attack rolls against you have 
Advantage, and your attack rolls have Disadvantage.
 Saving Throws Affected. You have Disadvantage 
on Dexterity saving throws.
full_text:
Restrained [Condition]
While you have the Restrained condition, you experience the following effects.
 Speed 0. Your Speed is 0 and can’t increase.
 Attacks Affected. Attack rolls against you have 
Advantage, and your attack rolls have Disadvantage.
 Saving Throws Affected. You have Disadvantage 
on Dexterity saving throws.

title: Stunned
summary: A stunned creature is incapacitated, cannot move, has impaired speech, fails Strength and Dexterity saves, and is easier to hit.
body: Attack rolls against a stunned creature have advantage. The condition is often short but very powerful because it denies actions and reactions.
tableUse: Use as a high-impact control condition; track duration carefully.
commonCalls: A stunned creature usually cannot take reactions.
Many stun effects repeat saves at end of turn.
srdText:
Stunned [Condition]
While you have the Stunned condition, you experience the following effects.
 Incapacitated. You have the Incapacitated 
condition.
 Saving Throws Affected. You automatically fail 
Strength and Dexterity saving throws.
 Attacks Affected. Attack rolls against you have 
Advantage.
full_text:
Stunned [Condition]
While you have the Stunned condition, you experience the following effects.
 Incapacitated. You have the Incapacitated 
condition.
 Saving Throws Affected. You automatically fail 
Strength and Dexterity saving throws.
 Attacks Affected. Attack rolls against you have 
Advantage.

title: Unconscious
summary: An unconscious creature is incapacitated, prone, unaware, drops held items, and is highly vulnerable to nearby attacks.
body: The creature cannot move or speak and is unaware. It drops what it holds and falls prone. Attacks against it have advantage, and hits from within 5 feet are critical hits.
tableUse: Use for sleep, knockout, and 0 hit point states when applicable.
commonCalls: A stable unconscious creature at 0 HP remains unconscious until healed or time passes.
Melee hits can rapidly cause death-save failures.
srdText:
Unconscious [Condition]
While you have the Unconscious condition, you experience the following effects.
 Inert. You have the Incapacitated and Prone 
conditions, and you drop whatever you’re holding. 
When this condition ends, you remain Prone.
 Speed 0. Your Speed is 0 and can’t increase.
 Attacks Affected. Attack rolls against you have 
Advantage.
 Saving Throws Affected. You automatically fail 
Strength and Dexterity saving throws.
 Automatic Critical Hits. Any attack roll that hits 
you is a Critical Hit if the attacker is within 5 feet of 
you.
 Unaware. You’re unaware of your surroundings.
full_text:
Unconscious [Condition]
While you have the Unconscious condition, you experience the following effects.
 Inert. You have the Incapacitated and Prone 
conditions, and you drop whatever you’re holding. 
When this condition ends, you remain Prone.
 Speed 0. Your Speed is 0 and can’t increase.
 Attacks Affected. Attack rolls against you have 
Advantage.
 Saving Throws Affected. You automatically fail 
Strength and Dexterity saving throws.
 Automatic Critical Hits. Any attack roll that hits 
you is a Critical Hit if the attacker is within 5 feet of 
you.
 Unaware. You’re unaware of your surroundings.

title: Dazed
summary: A Dazed creature can move or take an action on its turn - not both - and cannot take Bonus Actions or Reactions.
body:
While you have the Dazed condition:

- You can move or take one action on your turn, not both.
- You cannot take Bonus Actions.
- You cannot take Reactions.

The Dazed condition does not prevent you from speaking, perceiving your surroundings, or concentrating on a spell.
tableUse: Apply when a feature, spell, or trap description imposes the Dazed condition. It is a 2024 PHB condition - it does not exist as a named condition in the 2014 rules.
commonCalls: Dazed does not prevent movement entirely - the creature can still move OR act, just not both in the same turn.
No Bonus Actions and no Reactions while Dazed - this cuts action economy significantly.
Concentration checks are still made as normal - Dazed does not impose disadvantage on them by itself.
The condition ends when the effect that imposed it ends - check the source ability or spell for duration.
changeSummary: New formal condition introduced in the 2024 PHB. Has no direct 2014 equivalent - some 2014 abilities caused similar 'action or bonus action' restrictions but did not use this condition name.
dmImpact: This condition is 2024-only. If running 2014, treat similar language in monster abilities as a non-standard effect and describe it narratively.
dmSummary: A Dazed creature can move or take an action on its turn - not both - and cannot take Bonus Actions or Reactions.
nonSrdText:
While you have the Dazed condition:

- You can move or take one action on your turn, not both.
- You cannot take Bonus Actions.
- You cannot take Reactions.

The Dazed condition does not prevent you from speaking, perceiving your surroundings, or concentrating on a spell.
full_text:
While you have the Dazed condition:

- You can move or take one action on your turn, not both.
- You cannot take Bonus Actions.
- You cannot take Reactions.

The Dazed condition does not prevent you from speaking, perceiving your surroundings, or concentrating on a spell.

title: Silenced
summary: A Silenced creature cannot speak and cannot cast spells that require a Verbal component.
body:
While you have the Silenced condition:

- You cannot speak.
- You cannot cast spells that have a Verbal (V) component.

You can still take all other actions, move normally, make somatic gestures, and use material components. Silenced does not impose disadvantage on Perception checks by itself.
tableUse: Apply when a creature is within the area of a Silence spell or when an ability explicitly imposes the Silenced condition. In the 2024 rules this is a named formal condition rather than just a spell effect description.
commonCalls: Spells with only Somatic and/or Material components can still be cast while Silenced - only Verbal components are blocked.
Being Silenced does not prevent a creature from concentrating on a spell already in effect - it only blocks new casting with V components.
A creature with the Subtle Spell metamagic can cast V spells silently regardless - Silenced still blocks normal V spells.
Silenced is most commonly applied by the Silence spell. Creatures fully outside the Silence spell area are not Silenced even if adjacent.
In 2014, this was not a named condition - the Silence spell described its own area effect. Treat 2014 Silence spell zones the same way for practical purposes.
changeSummary: In 2014, silence was a spell-area effect described within the Silence spell text, not a formal named condition. The 2024 PHB formalizes it as a condition that can be applied by various abilities.
dmImpact: When running 2024 rules, any ability that says it 'silences' a target imposes the Silenced condition with all its defined effects. In 2014, only the Silence spell created this situation.
dmSummary: A Silenced creature cannot speak and cannot cast spells that require a Verbal component.
nonSrdText:
While you have the Silenced condition:

- You cannot speak.
- You cannot cast spells that have a Verbal (V) component.

You can still take all other actions, move normally, make somatic gestures, and use material components. Silenced does not impose disadvantage on Perception checks by itself.
full_text:
While you have the Silenced condition:

- You cannot speak.
- You cannot cast spells that have a Verbal (V) component.

You can still take all other actions, move normally, make somatic gestures, and use material components. Silenced does not impose disadvantage on Perception checks by itself.

title: Concentration
summary: Taking damage while concentrating requires a Constitution save: DC 10 or half the damage, whichever is higher.
body: Concentration ends if you start another concentration effect, become incapacitated, die, or fail a required save after damage. The 2024 Free Rules also cap the damage DC at 30.
tableUse: Call the save immediately after damage is applied; make separate saves for separate damage sources.
commonCalls: Readied concentration spells require concentration before release.
Environmental stress can call for concentration saves at the DM's discretion.
srdText:
Concentration
Some spells and other effects require Concentration 
to remain active, as specified in their descriptions. 
If the effect’s creator loses Concentration, the effect 
ends. If the effect has a maximum duration, the effect’s description specifies how long the creator can 
concentrate on it: up to 1 minute, 1 hour, or some 
other duration. The creator can end Concentration 
at any time (no action required). The following factors break Concentration.
 Another Concentration Effect. You lose Concentration on an effect the moment you start casting 
a spell that requires Concentration or activate another effect that requires Concentration.
 Damage. If you take damage, you must succeed 
on a Constitution saving throw to maintain Concentration. The DC equals 10 or half the damage taken 
(round down), whichever number is higher, up to a 
maximum DC of 30.
 Incapacitated or Dead. Your Concentration ends 
if you have the Incapacitated condition or you die.
full_text:
Concentration
Some spells and other effects require Concentration 
to remain active, as specified in their descriptions. 
If the effect’s creator loses Concentration, the effect 
ends. If the effect has a maximum duration, the effect’s description specifies how long the creator can 
concentrate on it: up to 1 minute, 1 hour, or some 
other duration. The creator can end Concentration 
at any time (no action required). The following factors break Concentration.

a spell that requires Concentration or activate another effect that requires Concentration.
 Damage. If you take damage, you must succeed 
on a Constitution saving throw to maintain Concentration. The DC equals 10 or half the damage taken 
(round down), whichever number is higher, up to a 
maximum DC of 30.
 Incapacitated or Dead. Your Concentration ends 
if you have the Incapacitated condition or you die.

title: Cover
summary: Cover protects targets: half cover grants +2 AC/Dex saves, three-quarters grants +5, and total cover blocks direct targeting.
body: Use cover when an obstacle, creature, wall, corner, or terrain feature blocks part or all of a target. Total cover usually prevents attacks or effects that require a direct target.
tableUse: Check the line from attacker or effect origin to target and apply the best cover that meaningfully blocks it.
commonCalls: Creatures can provide cover.
Area effects may ignore or interact with cover depending on origin and shape.
srdText:
Cover
Walls, trees, creatures, and other obstacles can provide cover, making a target more difficult to harm. 
As detailed in the Cover table, there are three degrees of cover, each of which gives a different benefit to a target.
 A target can benefit from cover only when an attack or other effect originates on the opposite side 
of the cover. If a target is behind multiple sources 
of cover, only the most protective degree of cover 
applies; the degrees aren’t added together. For example, if a target is behind a creature that gives Half 
Cover and a tree trunk that gives Three-Quarters 
Cover, the target has Three-Quarters Cover.
Cover
Degree Benefit to Target Offered By …
Half +2 bonus to AC 
and Dexterity 
saving throws
Another creature or 
an object that covers at 
least half of the target
Three- 
Quarters
+5 bonus to AC 
and Dexterity 
saving throws
An object that covers at 
least three-quarters of 
the target
Total Can’t be targeted 
directly
An object that covers 
the whole target

title: Death Saving Throws
summary: A player character at 0 HP rolls death saves at the start of each turn until stabilized, healed, or dead.
body: Track successes and failures. Three successes stabilizes; three failures kills. A natural 20 improves the situation, and a natural 1 counts more severely under the standard rules.
tableUse: Roll openly or secretly depending on table tension; track marks where everyone can see if desired.
commonCalls: Damage at 0 HP causes death-save failures.
Healing at any amount usually removes the character from death saves.
srdText:
Death Saving Throws
Whenever you start your turn with 0 Hit Points, 
you must make a Death Saving Throw to determine 
whether you creep closer to death or hang on to life. 
Unlike other saving throws, this one isn’t tied to an 
ability score. You’re in the hands of fate now.
 Three Successes/Failures. Roll 1d20. If the roll 
is 10 or higher, you succeed. Otherwise, you fail. A 
success or failure has no effect by itself. On your 
third success, you become Stable (see “Stabilizing a 
Character” below). On your third failure, you die.
 The successes and failures don’t need to be consecutive; keep track of both until you collect three 
of a kind. The number of both is reset to zero when 
you regain any Hit Points or become Stable.

 Rolling a 1 or 20. When you roll a 1 on the d20 
for a Death Saving Throw, you suffer two failures. If 
you roll a 20 on the d20, you regain 1 Hit Point.
 Damage at 0 Hit Points. If you take any damage 
while you have 0 Hit Points, you suffer a Death Saving Throw failure. If the damage is from a Critical 
Hit, you suffer two failures instead. If the damage 
equals or exceeds your Hit Point maximum, you die.

Death Saving Throws
Whenever you start your turn with 0 Hit Points, 
you must make a Death Saving Throw to determine 
whether you creep closer to death or hang on to life. 
Unlike other saving throws, this one isn’t tied to an

success or failure has no effect by itself. On your 
third success, you become Stable (see “Stabilizing a 
Character” below). On your third failure, you die.
 The successes and failures don’t need to be consecutive; keep track of both until you collect three 
of a kind. The number of both is reset to zero when 
you regain any Hit Points or become Stable.

 Rolling a 1 or 20. When you roll a 1 on the d20 
for a Death Saving Throw, you suffer two failures. If 
you roll a 20 on the d20, you regain 1 Hit Point.
 Damage at 0 Hit Points. If you take any damage 
while you have 0 Hit Points, you suffer a Death Saving Throw failure. If the damage is from a Critical 
Hit, you suffer two failures instead. If the damage 
equals or exceeds your Hit Point maximum, you die.

title: Stabilizing a Creature
summary: A dying creature can be stabilized, ending death saves without restoring hit points.
body: A successful Medicine check or appropriate healing feature can stabilize a creature at 0 HP. A stable creature remains unconscious until healed or enough time passes by the applicable rule.
tableUse: Let allies spend actions and resources to stop death-save pressure.
commonCalls: A healer's kit or magic can bypass or improve the check depending on rules used.
srdText:
Stabilizing a Character
You can take the Help action to try to stabilize a 
creature with 0 Hit Points, which requires a successful DC 10 Wisdom (Medicine) check.
 A Stable creature doesn’t make Death Saving 
Throws even though it has 0 Hit Points, but it still 
has the Unconscious condition. If the creature takes 
damage, it stops being Stable and starts making 
Death Saving Throws again. A Stable creature that 
isn’t healed regains 1 Hit Point after 1d4 hours.
full_text:
Stabilizing a Character
You can take the Help action to try to stabilize a 
creature with 0 Hit Points, which requires a successful DC 10 Wisdom (Medicine) check.
 A Stable creature doesn’t make Death Saving 
Throws even though it has 0 Hit Points, but it still 
has the Unconscious condition. If the creature takes 
damage, it stops being Stable and starts making 
Death Saving Throws again. A Stable creature that 
isn’t healed regains 1 Hit Point after 1d4 hours.

title: Short Rest
summary: A short rest is a brief downtime period where characters can spend Hit Dice and recover some features.
body: The rest normally takes about 1 hour. Characters may spend Hit Dice for healing, and features that recover on a short rest refresh when the rest is completed.
tableUse: Use to pace adventuring days and let the party recover without a full reset.
commonCalls: Interruptions may prevent the rest.
Warlock and class-resource recovery can vary by edition and class text.
srdText: Short Rest A Short Rest is a 1-hour period of downtime, during which a creature does nothing more strenuous than reading, talking, eating, or standing watch. To start a Short Rest, you must have at least 1 Hit Point. Benefits of the Rest. When you finish the rest, you gain the following benefits: Spend Hit Point Dice. You can spend one or more of your Hit Point Dice to regain Hit Points. For each Hit Point Die you spend in this way, roll the die and add your Constitution modifier to it. You regain Hit Points equal to the total (minimum of 1 Hit Point). You can decide to spend an additional Hit Point Die after each roll. Special Feature. Some features are recharged by a Short Rest. If you have such a feature, it recharges in the way specified in its description. Interrupting the Rest. A Short Rest is stopped by the following interruptions: • Rolling Initiative • Casting a spell other than a cantrip • Taking any damage An interrupted Short Rest confers no benefits.
full_text: Short Rest A Short Rest is a 1-hour period of downtime, during which a creature does nothing more strenuous than reading, talking, eating, or standing watch. To start a Short Rest, you must have at least 1 Hit Point. Benefits of the Rest. When you finish the rest, you gain the following benefits: Spend Hit Point Dice. You can spend one or more of your Hit Point Dice to regain Hit Points. For each Hit Point Die you spend in this way, roll the die and add your Constitution modifier to it. You regain Hit Points equal to the total (minimum of 1 Hit Point). You can decide to spend an additional Hit Point Die after each roll. Special Feature. Some features are recharged by a Short Rest. If you have such a feature, it recharges in the way specified in its description. Interrupting the Rest. A Short Rest is stopped by the following interruptions: • Rolling Initiative • Casting a spell other than a cantrip • Taking any damage An interrupted Short Rest confers no benefits.

title: Long Rest
summary: A long rest is extended downtime that restores hit points, some Hit Dice, and most daily resources.
body: A long rest usually requires several hours of rest and limited interruption. It restores all hit points and recovers expended Hit Dice up to the edition's limit, along with features that recharge on a long rest.
tableUse: Use to mark the end of an adventuring push or safe recovery period.
commonCalls: A creature usually benefits from only one long rest in a 24-hour period.
Resting in unsafe conditions may be interrupted.
srdText: Long Rest A Long Rest is a period of extended downtime-at least 8 hours-available to any creature. During a Long Rest, you sleep for at least 6 hours and perform no more than 2 hours of light activity, such as reading, talking, eating, or standing watch. During sleep, you have the Unconscious condition. After you finish a Long Rest, you must wait at least 16 hours before starting another one. Benefits of the Rest. To start a Long Rest, you must have at least 1 Hit Point. When you finish the rest, you gain the following benefits: Regain All HP. You regain all lost Hit Points and all spent Hit Point Dice. If your Hit Point maximum was reduced, it returns to normal. Ability Scores Restored. If any of your ability scores were reduced, they return to normal. Exhaustion Reduced. If you have the Exhaustion condition, its level decreases by 1. Special Feature. Some features are recharged by a Long Rest. If you have such a feature, it recharges in the way specified in its description. Interrupting the Rest. A Long Rest is stopped by the following interruptions: • Rolling Initiative • Casting a spell other than a cantrip • Taking any damage • 1 hour of walking or other physical exertion If you rested at least 1 hour before the interruption, you gain the benefits of a Short Rest. See also “Short Rest.” You can resume a Long Rest immediately after an interruption. If you do so, the rest requires 1 additional hour per interruption to finish.
full_text: Long Rest A Long Rest is a period of extended downtime-at least 8 hours-available to any creature. During a Long Rest, you sleep for at least 6 hours and perform no more than 2 hours of light activity, such as reading, talking, eating, or standing watch. During sleep, you have the Unconscious condition. After you finish a Long Rest, you must wait at least 16 hours before starting another one. Benefits of the Rest. To start a Long Rest, you must have at least 1 Hit Point. When you finish the rest, you gain the following benefits: Regain All HP. You regain all lost Hit Points and all spent Hit Point Dice. If your Hit Point maximum was reduced, it returns to normal. Ability Scores Restored. If any of your ability scores were reduced, they return to normal. Exhaustion Reduced. If you have the Exhaustion condition, its level decreases by 1. Special Feature. Some features are recharged by a Long Rest. If you have such a feature, it recharges in the way specified in its description. Interrupting the Rest. A Long Rest is stopped by the following interruptions: • Rolling Initiative • Casting a spell other than a cantrip • Taking any damage • 1 hour of walking or other physical exertion If you rested at least 1 hour before the interruption, you gain the benefits of a Short Rest. See also “Short Rest.” You can resume a Long Rest immediately after an interruption. If you do so, the rest requires 1 additional hour per interruption to finish.

title: Healing
summary: Healing restores hit points up to the target's maximum and can bring a creature at 0 HP back into the fight.
body: A creature cannot exceed its hit point maximum through ordinary healing. Healing a dying creature usually ends death saving throws and returns it to consciousness if HP rises above 0.
tableUse: Apply healing after damage and condition effects are resolved unless the triggering rule says otherwise.
commonCalls: Temporary hit points are not healing.
Undead or constructs may interact differently with specific healing effects.
srdText:
Healing
Hit Points can be restored by magic, such as the 
Cure Wounds spell or a Potion of Healing, or by a 
Short or Long Rest (see “Rules Glossary”).
 When you receive healing, add the restored Hit 
Points to your current Hit Points. Your Hit Points 
can’t exceed your Hit Point maximum, so any Hit 
Points regained in excess of the maximum are lost. 
For example, if you receive 8 Hit Points of healing 
and have 14 Hit Points and a Hit Point maximum of 
20, you regain 6 Hit Points, not 8.
full_text:
Healing
Hit Points can be restored by magic, such as the 
Cure Wounds spell or a Potion of Healing, or by a 
Short or Long Rest (see “Rules Glossary”).
 When you receive healing, add the restored Hit 
Points to your current Hit Points. Your Hit Points 
can’t exceed your Hit Point maximum, so any Hit 
Points regained in excess of the maximum are lost. 
For example, if you receive 8 Hit Points of healing 
and have 14 Hit Points and a Hit Point maximum of 
20, you regain 6 Hit Points, not 8.

title: Falling
summary: A falling creature usually takes bludgeoning damage based on distance fallen and lands prone.
body: The common rule is 1d6 bludgeoning damage per 10 feet fallen, to a maximum of 20d6, and the creature lands prone unless a rule prevents it.
tableUse: Apply when a creature drops, is pushed, loses flight, or fails a climb.
commonCalls: Feather fall and similar effects override this.
Flying creatures may have special rules if knocked prone.
srdText: Falling [Hazard] A creature that falls takes 1d6 Bludgeoning damage at the end of the fall for every 10 feet it fell, to a maximum of 20d6. When the creature lands, it has the Prone condition unless it avoids taking any damage from the fall. A creature that falls into water or another liquid can use its Reaction to make a DC 15 Strength (Athletics) or Dexterity (Acrobatics) check to hit the surface head or feet first. On a successful check, any damage resulting from the fall is halved.
full_text: Falling [Hazard] A creature that falls takes 1d6 Bludgeoning damage at the end of the fall for every 10 feet it fell, to a maximum of 20d6. When the creature lands, it has the Prone condition unless it avoids taking any damage from the fall. A creature that falls into water or another liquid can use its Reaction to make a DC 15 Strength (Athletics) or Dexterity (Acrobatics) check to hit the surface head or feet first. On a successful check, any damage resulting from the fall is halved.

title: Suffocation
summary: A creature can hold its breath for a limited time, then suffers severe consequences when it runs out of air.
body: Breath-holding is based on Constitution. In the 2024 Free Rules, suffocation applies exhaustion levels at the end of turns until the creature can breathe again, then removes the suffocation-caused levels.
tableUse: Use for drowning, choking, vacuum, smoke, or buried characters.
commonCalls: Track rounds once breath runs out.
Species and magic can modify breathing requirements.
srdText: Suffocation [Hazard] A creature can hold its breath for a number of minutes equal to 1 plus its Constitution modifier (minimum of 30 seconds) before suffocation begins. When a creature runs out of breath or is choking, it gains 1 Exhaustion level at the end of each of its turns. When a creature can breathe again, it removes all levels of Exhaustion it gained from suffocating.
full_text: Suffocation [Hazard] A creature can hold its breath for a number of minutes equal to 1 plus its Constitution modifier (minimum of 30 seconds) before suffocation begins. When a creature runs out of breath or is choking, it gains 1 Exhaustion level at the end of each of its turns. When a creature can breathe again, it removes all levels of Exhaustion it gained from suffocating.

title: Jumping
summary: Jump distance is based mainly on Strength, with running starts improving both long and high jumps.
body: Long jumps generally use Strength score as the running jump distance in feet. High jumps generally use 3 plus Strength modifier with a running start, with shorter jumps from standing starts.
tableUse: Use checks only when the jump is risky, obstructed, contested, or beyond normal capability.
commonCalls: Jumping still spends movement.
The DM can call for Athletics for awkward landings or extra distance.
srdText: Jumping When you jump, you make either a Long Jump (horizontal) or a High Jump (vertical). See also “Long Jump” and “High Jump.”
full_text: Jumping When you jump, you make either a Long Jump (horizontal) or a High Jump (vertical). See also “Long Jump” and “High Jump.”

title: Grappling
summary: Grappling restrains movement by applying the Grappled condition, but the procedure differs by edition.
body: 2014 commonly uses a special melee attack contested by Athletics or Acrobatics. 2024 reorganizes grappling through Unarmed Strike options and saving throws. In both cases, the result mainly stops movement.
tableUse: Use to hold enemies in hazards, stop retreat, or control positioning.
commonCalls: Grappled is not restrained.
Forced separation can end the grapple.
srdText: Grappling A creature can grapple another creature. Characters typically grapple by using an Unarmed Strike. Many monsters have special attacks that allow them to quickly grapple prey. However a grapple is initiated, it follows these rules. See also “Unarmed Strike” and “Grappled.” Grappled Condition. Successfully grappling a creature gives it the Grappled condition. One Grapple per Hand. A creature must have a hand free to grapple another creature. Some stat blocks and game effects allow a creature to grapple using a tentacle, a maw, or another body part. Whatever part a grappler uses, it can grapple only one creature at a time with that part, and the grappler can’t use that part to target another creature unless it ends the grapple. Ending a Grapple. A Grappled creature can use its action to make a Strength (Athletics) or Dexterity (Acrobatics) check against the grapple’s escape DC, ending the condition on itself on a success. The condition also ends if the grappler has the Incapacitated condition or if the distance between the Grappled target and the grappler exceeds the grapple’s range. In addition, the grappler can release the target at any time (no action required).
full_text: Grappling A creature can grapple another creature. Characters typically grapple by using an Unarmed Strike. Many monsters have special attacks that allow them to quickly grapple prey. However a grapple is initiated, it follows these rules. See also “Unarmed Strike” and “Grappled.” Grappled Condition. Successfully grappling a creature gives it the Grappled condition. One Grapple per Hand. A creature must have a hand free to grapple another creature. Some stat blocks and game effects allow a creature to grapple using a tentacle, a maw, or another body part. Whatever part a grappler uses, it can grapple only one creature at a time with that part, and the grappler can’t use that part to target another creature unless it ends the grapple. Ending a Grapple. A Grappled creature can use its action to make a Strength (Athletics) or Dexterity (Acrobatics) check against the grapple’s escape DC, ending the condition on itself on a success. The condition also ends if the grappler has the Incapacitated condition or if the distance between the Grappled target and the grappler exceeds the grapple’s range. In addition, the grappler can release the target at any time (no action required).

title: Shoving
summary: A shove pushes a target or knocks it prone, with exact procedure differing by edition.
body: 2014 treats shove as a special melee attack with a contest. 2024 routes shove through Unarmed Strike options and saving throw language. The outcome is usually forced movement or prone.
tableUse: Use near ledges, hazards, chokepoints, and for teamwork advantage.
commonCalls: Size limits usually apply.
A shove can break formations or expose a target to opportunity attacks indirectly.
srdText: Unarmed Strike Instead of using a weapon to make a melee attack, you can use a punch, kick, headbutt, or similar forceful blow. In game terms, this is an Unarmed Strike-a melee attack that involves you using your body to damage, grapple, or shove a target within 5 feet of you. Whenever you use your Unarmed Strike, choose one of the following options for its effect. Damage. You make an attack roll against the target. Your bonus to the roll equals your Strength modifier plus your Proficiency Bonus. On a hit, the target takes Bludgeoning damage equal to 1 plus your Strength modifier. Grapple. The target must succeed on a Strength or Dexterity saving throw (it chooses which), or it has the Grappled condition. The DC for the saving throw and any escape attempts equals 8 plus your Strength modifier and Proficiency Bonus. This grapple is possible only if the target is no more than one size larger than you and if you have a hand free to grab it. See also “Grappling.” Shove. The target must succeed on a Strength or Dexterity saving throw (it chooses which), or you either push it 5 feet away or cause it to have the Prone condition. The DC for the saving throw equals 8 plus your Strength modifier and Proficiency Bonus. This shove is possible only if the target is no more than one size larger than you.
full_text: Unarmed Strike Instead of using a weapon to make a melee attack, you can use a punch, kick, headbutt, or similar forceful blow. In game terms, this is an Unarmed Strike-a melee attack that involves you using your body to damage, grapple, or shove a target within 5 feet of you. Whenever you use your Unarmed Strike, choose one of the following options for its effect. Damage. You make an attack roll against the target. Your bonus to the roll equals your Strength modifier plus your Proficiency Bonus. On a hit, the target takes Bludgeoning damage equal to 1 plus your Strength modifier. Grapple. The target must succeed on a Strength or Dexterity saving throw (it chooses which), or it has the Grappled condition. The DC for the saving throw and any escape attempts equals 8 plus your Strength modifier and Proficiency Bonus. This grapple is possible only if the target is no more than one size larger than you and if you have a hand free to grab it. See also “Grappling.” Shove. The target must succeed on a Strength or Dexterity saving throw (it chooses which), or you either push it 5 feet away or cause it to have the Prone condition. The DC for the saving throw equals 8 plus your Strength modifier and Proficiency Bonus. This shove is possible only if the target is no more than one size larger than you.

title: Difficult Terrain
summary: Each foot of movement through difficult terrain costs 1 extra foot.
body: Difficult terrain represents rubble, undergrowth, steep stairs, ice, mud, crowded spaces, or similar impediments.
tableUse: Use to make positioning matter without adding new rolls.
commonCalls: Multiple difficult-terrain effects usually do not stack unless a rule says so.
Crawling through difficult terrain can become very expensive.
srdText: Difficult Terrain If a space is Difficult Terrain, every foot of movement in that space costs 1 extra foot. For example, moving 5 feet through Difficult Terrain costs 10 feet of movement. Difficult Terrain isn’t cumulative; either a space is Difficult Terrain or it isn’t. A space is Difficult Terrain if the space contains any of the following or something similar: • A creature that isn’t Tiny or your ally • Furniture that is sized for creatures of your size or larger • Heavy snow, ice, rubble, or undergrowth • Liquid that’s between shin- and waist-deep • A narrow opening sized for a creature one size smaller than you • A slope of 20 degrees or more
full_text: Difficult Terrain If a space is Difficult Terrain, every foot of movement in that space costs 1 extra foot. For example, moving 5 feet through Difficult Terrain costs 10 feet of movement. Difficult Terrain isn’t cumulative; either a space is Difficult Terrain or it isn’t. A space is Difficult Terrain if the space contains any of the following or something similar: • A creature that isn’t Tiny or your ally • Furniture that is sized for creatures of your size or larger • Heavy snow, ice, rubble, or undergrowth • Liquid that’s between shin- and waist-deep • A narrow opening sized for a creature one size smaller than you • A slope of 20 degrees or more

title: Movement
summary: On your turn, you can move up to your Speed, breaking movement up before and after actions as allowed.
body: Movement is measured in feet and can be split around actions, bonus actions, and interactions. Terrain, conditions, climbing, swimming, crawling, and features can change the cost.
tableUse: Track exact squares or rough distances depending on tactical detail needed.
commonCalls: Speed 0 prevents movement unless a rule moves you.
Standing from prone costs movement.
srdText:
Movement and Position
On your turn, you can move a distance equal to your 
Speed or less. Or you can decide not to move.
 Your movement can include climbing, crawling, 
jumping, and swimming (each explained in “Rules 
Glossary”). These different modes of movement can 
be combined with your regular movement, or they 
can constitute your entire move.
 However you’re moving with your Speed, you 
deduct the distance of each part of your move from 
it until it is used up or until you are done moving, 
whichever comes first.
 A character’s Speed is determined during character creation. A monster’s Speed is noted in the 
monster’s stat block. See “Rules Glossary” for more 
about Speed as well as about special speeds, such as 
a Climb Speed, Fly Speed, or Swim Speed.
Difficult Terrain
Combatants are often slowed down by Difficult 
Terrain. Low furniture, rubble, undergrowth, steep 
stairs, snow, and shallow bogs are examples of Difficult Terrain.
 Every foot of movement in Difficult Terrain costs 
1 extra foot, even if multiple things in a space count 
as Difficult Terrain.
Breaking Up Your Move
You can break up your move, using some of its 
movement before and after any action, Bonus Action, or Reaction you take on the same turn. For 
example, if you have a Speed of 30 feet, you could go 
10 feet, take an action, and then go 20 feet.
Dropping Prone
On your turn, you can give yourself the Prone condition (see “Rules Glossary”) without using an action 
or any of your Speed, but you can’t do so if your 
Speed is 0.
Creature Size
A creature belongs to a size category, which determines the width of the square space the creature 
occupies on a map, as shown on the Creature Size 
and Space table. That table lists the sizes from 
smallest (Tiny) to largest (Gargantuan). A creature’s 
space is the area that it effectively controls in combat and the area it needs to fight effectively.
 A character’s size is determined by species, and 
a monster’s size is specified in the monster’s stat 
block.
Creature Size and Space
Size Space (Feet) Space (Squares)
Tiny 2½ by 2½ feet 4 per square
Small 5 by 5 feet 1 square
Medium 5 by 5 feet 1 square
Large 10 by 10 feet 4 squares (2 by 2)
Huge 15 by 15 feet 9 squares (3 by 3)
Gargantuan 20 by 20 feet 16 squares (4 by 4)
Moving around Other Creatures
During your move, you can pass through the space 
of an ally, a creature that has the Incapacitated 
condition (see “Rules Glossary”), a Tiny creature, or 
a creature that is two sizes larger or smaller than 
you.
 Another creature’s space is Difficult Terrain for 
you unless that creature is Tiny or your ally.
 You can’t willingly end a move in a space occupied 
by another creature. If you somehow end a turn in 
a space with another creature, you have the Prone 
condition (see “Rules Glossary”) unless you are Tiny 
or are of a larger size than the other creature.

title: Mounted Combat
summary: A rider can use a suitable mount for faster movement, with control and initiative rules depending on whether the mount is controlled or independent.
body: A controlled mount is usually limited to basic movement actions, while an independent mount acts more like its own creature. Being knocked prone or moved can force checks or dismounts.
tableUse: Decide whether the mount is controlled, independent, trained, or acting on instinct.
commonCalls: Mount size and anatomy matter.
Opportunity attacks can involve rider or mount depending on positioning and rule text.
srdText: Mounted Combat A knight charging into battle on a warhorse, a wizard casting spells from the back of a griffon, or a cleric soaring through the sky on a pegasus all enjoy the benefits of speed and mobility that a mount can provide. A willing creature that is at least one size larger than you and that has an appropriate anatomy can serve as a mount, using the following rules. Mounting and Dismounting Once during your move, you can mount a creature that is within 5 feet of you or dismount. Doing so costs an amount of movement equal to half your speed. For example, if your speed is 30 feet, you must spend 15 feet of movement to mount a horse. Therefore, you can’t mount it if you don’t have 15 feet of movement left or if your speed is 0. If an effect moves your mount against its will while you’re on it, you must succeed on a DC 10 Dexterity saving throw or fall off the mount, landing prone in a space within 5 feet of it. If you’re knocked prone while mounted, you must make the same saving throw. If your mount is knocked prone, you can use your reaction to dismount it as it falls and land on your feet. Otherwise, you are dismounted and fall prone in a space within 5 feet of it. Controlling a Mount While you’re mounted, you have two options. You can either control the mount or allow it to act independently. Intelligent creatures, such as dragons, act independently. You can control a mount only if it has been trained to accept a rider. Domesticated horses, donkeys, and similar creatures are assumed to have such training. The initiative of a controlled mount changes to match yours when you mount it. It moves as you direct it, and it has only three action options: Dash, Disengage, and Dodge. A controlled mount can move and act even on the turn that you mount it. An independent mount retains its place in the initiative order. Bearing a rider puts no restrictions on the actions the mount can take, and it moves and acts as it wishes. It might flee from combat, rush to attack and devour a badly injured foe, or otherwise act against your wishes. In either case, if the mount provokes an opportunity attack while you’re on it, the attacker can target you or the mount.

If an effect moves your mount against its will while you’re on it, you must succeed on a DC 10 Dexterity saving throw or fall off the mount, landing prone in a space within 5 feet of it. If you’re knocked prone while mounted, you must make the same saving throw. If your mount is knocked prone, you can use your reaction to dismount it as it falls and land on your feet. Otherwise, you are dismounted and fall prone in a space within 5 feet of it. Controlling a Mount While you’re mounted, you have two options. You can either control the mount or allow it to act independently. Intelligent creatures, such as dragons, act independently. You can control a mount only if it has been trained to accept a rider.
