It might flee from combat, rush to attack and devour a badly injured foe, or otherwise act against your wishes. In either case, if the mount provokes an opportunity attack while you’re on it, the attacker can target you or the mount.

title: Underwater Combat
summary: Underwater fighting changes movement, attack reliability, fire effects, and breathing pressure.
body: Creatures without swim speeds spend extra movement swimming. Some weapon attacks are impaired underwater unless the weapon is suitable. Fire and breathing rules often matter.
tableUse: Use when water is deep enough to affect footing, line of sight, attacks, or breath.
commonCalls: Piercing weapons are usually more reliable underwater.
Swimming creatures or magic can bypass many penalties.
srdText: Underwater Combat When adventurers pursue sahuagin back to their undersea homes, fight off sharks in an ancient shipwreck, or find themselves in a flooded dungeon room, they must fight in a challenging environment. Underwater the following rules apply. When making a melee weapon attack, a creature that doesn’t have a swimming speed (either natural or granted by magic) has disadvantage on the attack roll unless the weapon is a dagger, javelin, shortsword, spear, or trident. A ranged weapon attack automatically misses a target beyond the weapon’s normal range. Even against a target within normal range, the attack roll has disadvantage unless the weapon is a crossbow, a net, or a weapon that is thrown like a javelin (including a spear, trident, or dart). Creatures and objects that are fully immersed in water have resistance to fire damage.
full_text: Underwater Combat When adventurers pursue sahuagin back to their undersea homes, fight off sharks in an ancient shipwreck, or find themselves in a flooded dungeon room, they must fight in a challenging environment. Underwater the following rules apply. When making a melee weapon attack, a creature that doesn’t have a swimming speed (either natural or granted by magic) has disadvantage on the attack roll unless the weapon is a dagger, javelin, shortsword, spear, or trident. A ranged weapon attack automatically misses a target beyond the weapon’s normal range. Even against a target within normal range, the attack roll has disadvantage unless the weapon is a crossbow, a net, or a weapon that is thrown like a javelin (including a spear, trident, or dart). Creatures and objects that are fully immersed in water have resistance to fire damage.

title: Spell Attack Rolls
summary: A spell attack roll uses d20 plus the caster's spell attack modifier against the target AC.
body: Spell attack modifier is usually spellcasting ability modifier plus proficiency bonus, plus any special bonuses. The spell says whether it is melee or ranged.
tableUse: Use for spells that say to make a spell attack rather than forcing a saving throw.
commonCalls: Ranged spell attacks can be affected by close enemies and cover.
Critical hits can apply to spell attack damage.
srdText:
Attack Rolls
Some spells require the caster to make an attack 
roll to determine whether the spell hits a target. 
Here’s how to calculate the attack modifier for your 
spells:
Spell attack modifier = your spellcasting ability 
modifier + your Proficiency Bonus
full_text:
Attack Rolls
Some spells require the caster to make an attack 
roll to determine whether the spell hits a target. 
Here’s how to calculate the attack modifier for your 
spells:
Spell attack modifier = your spellcasting ability 
modifier + your Proficiency Bonus

title: Spell Save DC
summary: A caster's spell save DC is the number targets must meet or beat when a spell calls for a saving throw.
body: The standard formula is 8 plus proficiency bonus plus the caster's spellcasting ability modifier, plus any special bonuses.
tableUse: Use whenever a spell asks a target to make a saving throw.
commonCalls: The spell states which ability is used for the save.
Cover can improve Dexterity saves against some effects.
srdText:
Saving Throws
Many spells specify that a target makes a saving 
throw to avoid some or all of a spell’s effects. The 
spell specifies the ability that the target uses for 
the save and what happens on a success or failure. 
Here’s how to calculate the DC for your spells:
Spell save DC = 8 + your spellcasting ability modifier 
+ your Proficiency Bonus
full_text:

+ your Proficiency Bonus

title: Spell Components
summary: Spells can require verbal, somatic, and material components; lacking a required component can prevent casting.
body: Verbal components require speaking, somatic components require gestures, and material components require specified materials or a focus unless the component is consumed or has a cost requirement.
tableUse: Check components when hands are occupied, speech is blocked, gear is missing, or stealth matters.
commonCalls: A focus usually replaces non-costly, non-consumed material components.
Silence-like effects can block verbal spells.
srdText:
Components
A spell’s components are physical requirements the 
spellcaster must meet to cast the spell. Each spell’s 
description indicates whether it requires Verbal 
(V), Somatic (S), or Material (M) components. If the 
spellcaster can’t provide one or more of a spell’s 
components, the spellcaster can’t cast the spell.
Verbal (V)
A Verbal component is the chanting of esoteric 
words that sound like nonsense to the uninitiated. 
The words must be uttered in a normal speaking 
voice. The words themselves aren’t the source of the 
spell’s power; rather, the particular combination of 
sounds, with specific pitch and resonance, sets the 
threads of magic in motion. Thus, a creature who is 
gagged or in an area of magical silence can’t cast a 
spell with a Verbal component.
Somatic (S)
A Somatic component is a forceful gesticulation 
or an intricate set of gestures. A spellcaster must 
use at least one of their hands to perform these 
movements.
Material (M)
A Material component is a particular material used 
in a spell’s casting, as specified in parentheses in 
the Components entry. These materials aren’t consumed by the spell unless the spell’s description 
states otherwise. The spellcaster must have a hand 
free to access them, but it can be the same hand 
used to perform Somatic components, if any.
 If a spell doesn’t consume its materials and 
doesn’t specify a cost for them, a spellcaster can use 
a Component Pouch (see “Equipment”) instead of 
providing the materials specified in the spell, or the 

id Enti Fyin G an o n Goin G spEll
You can try to identify a non-instantaneous spell by 
its observable effects if its duration is ongoing. To 
identify it, you must take the Study action and succeed on a DC 15 Intelligence (Arcana) check.
spellcaster can substitute a Spellcasting Focus if the 
caster has a feature that allows that substitution. To 
use a Component Pouch, you must have a hand free 
to reach into it, and to use a Spellcasting Focus, you 
must hold it unless its description says otherwise 
(see “Equipment” for descriptions).

title: Concentration Spells
summary: You can concentrate on only one concentration effect at a time.
body: Casting or activating another concentration effect ends the first one. Damage, incapacitation, death, or special environmental stress can also end concentration.
tableUse: Ask players to name which concentration effect remains whenever they cast another.
commonCalls: Concentration duration is a maximum, not a guaranteed duration.
Readied spells can require concentration before release.
srdText:
Concentration
Some spells and other effects require Concentration 
to remain active, as specified in their descriptions. 
If the effect’s creator loses Concentration, the effect 
ends. If the effect has a maximum duration, the effect’s description specifies how long the creator can 
concentrate on it: up to 1 minute, 1 hour, or some 
other duration. The creator can end Concentration 
at any time (no action required). The following factors break Concentration.
 Another Concentration Effect. You lose Concentration on an effect the moment you start casting 
a spell that requires Concentration or activate another effect that requires Concentration.
 Damage. If you take damage, you must succeed 
on a Constitution saving throw to maintain Concentration. The DC equals 10 or half the damage taken 
(round down), whichever number is higher, up to a 
maximum DC of 30.
 Incapacitated or Dead. Your Concentration ends 
if you have the Incapacitated condition or you die.

 Incapacitated or Dead. Your Concentration ends 
if you have the Incapacitated condition or you die.

title: Ritual Casting
summary: Some spells can be cast as rituals, taking extra time but not spending a spell slot if the caster has ritual access.
body: A spell must have the ritual tag and the caster must have a feature that allows ritual casting. Ritual casting usually adds 10 minutes to casting time.
tableUse: Use outside immediate danger when the party has time but wants to conserve slots.
commonCalls: Not every caster can ritual-cast every ritual spell.
Interruptions can spoil the casting.
srdText:
Ritual
If you have a spell prepared that has the Ritual tag, 
you can cast that spell as a Ritual. The Ritual version of a spell takes 10 minutes longer to cast than 
normal. It also doesn’t expend a spell slot, which 
means the ritual version of a spell can’t be cast at a 
higher level. See also “Spells.”
full_text:
Ritual
If you have a spell prepared that has the Ritual tag, 
you can cast that spell as a Ritual. The Ritual version of a spell takes 10 minutes longer to cast than 
normal. It also doesn’t expend a spell slot, which 
means the ritual version of a spell can’t be cast at a 
higher level. See also “Spells.”

title: Bonus Action Spells
summary: Casting a spell as a bonus action restricts what other spells can be cast on the same turn.
body: The classic 2014 rule allows only a cantrip with a casting time of 1 action as the other spell on that turn. The 2024 presentation clarifies timing and should be checked against current spellcasting text.
tableUse: When a player casts a bonus-action spell, immediately check what other spell they want to cast that turn.
commonCalls: Reaction spells on another turn are usually separate.
Magic item activation may use different wording.
srdText: Casting Time Most spells require the Magic action to cast, but some spells require a Bonus Action, a Reaction, or 1 minute or more. A spell’s Casting Time entry specifies which of those is required. One Spell with a Spell Slot per Turn On a turn, you can expend only one spell slot to cast a spell. This rule means you can’t, for example, cast a spell with a spell slot using the Magic action and another one using a Bonus Action on the same turn. Reaction and Bonus Action Triggers A spell that has a casting time of a Reaction is cast in response to a trigger that is defined in the spell’s Casting Time entry. Some spells that have a casting time of a Bonus Action are also cast in response to a trigger defined in the spell. Longer Casting Times Certain spells-including a spell cast as a Ritual-require more time to cast: minutes or even hours. While you cast a spell with a casting time of 1 minute or more, you must take the Magic action on each of your turns, and you must maintain Concentration (see “Rules Glossary”) while you do so. If your Concentration is broken, the spell fails, but you don’t expend a spell slot. To cast the spell again, you must start over.
full_text: Casting Time Most spells require the Magic action to cast, but some spells require a Bonus Action, a Reaction, or 1 minute or more. A spell’s Casting Time entry specifies which of those is required. One Spell with a Spell Slot per Turn On a turn, you can expend only one spell slot to cast a spell. This rule means you can’t, for example, cast a spell with a spell slot using the Magic action and another one using a Bonus Action on the same turn. Reaction and Bonus Action Triggers A spell that has a casting time of a Reaction is cast in response to a trigger that is defined in the spell’s Casting Time entry. Some spells that have a casting time of a Bonus Action are also cast in response to a trigger defined in the spell. Longer Casting Times Certain spells-including a spell cast as a Ritual-require more time to cast: minutes or even hours. While you cast a spell with a casting time of 1 minute or more, you must take the Magic action on each of your turns, and you must maintain Concentration (see “Rules Glossary”) while you do so. If your Concentration is broken, the spell fails, but you don’t expend a spell slot. To cast the spell again, you must start over.

title: Areas of Effect
summary: Area effects define a shape, origin, size, and affected space; creatures in that area resolve the spell or effect.
body: Common areas include cone, cube, cylinder, line, sphere, and newer 2024 glossary terms such as emanation. The effect text defines origin, range, and whether the origin is included.
tableUse: Place the origin first, then trace the shape and check cover or line rules as needed.
commonCalls: Grid play often needs a consistent table convention.
Total cover can block some effects depending on origin and wording.
srdText:
Area of Effect
The descriptions of many spells and other features 
specify that they have an area of effect, which typically has one of six shapes. These shapes are defined elsewhere in this glossary:
Cone
Cube
Cylinder
Emanation
Line
Sphere
An area of effect has a point of origin, a location 
from which the effect’s energy erupts. The rules for 
each shape specify how to position its point of origin. If all straight lines extending from the point of 
origin to a location in the area of effect are blocked, 
that location isn’t included in the area of effect. 
To block a line, an obstruction must provide Total 
Cover. See also “Cover.”
 If the creator of an area of effect places it at an 
unseen point and an obstruction—such as a wall—
is between the creator and that point, the point 
of origin comes into being on the near side of the 
obstruction.

title: Bright Light
summary: Bright light lets most creatures see normally.
body: Sunlight, torches, lanterns, and many spells create bright light in a specified radius. Normal sight works in bright light unless another effect interferes.
tableUse: Use as the default lighting state when visibility is not meaningfully impaired.
commonCalls: Bright light can matter for features sensitive to sunlight or darkness.
srdSection: SRD 5.2 > Playing the Game > Exploration > Vision and Light > Bright Light
srdText: Bright Light. Bright Light lets most creatures see normally. Even gloomy days provide Bright Light, as do torches, lanterns, fires, and other sources of illumination within a specific radius.
full_text: Bright Light. Bright Light lets most creatures see normally. Even gloomy days provide Bright Light, as do torches, lanterns, fires, and other sources of illumination within a specific radius.
page_ref: SRD 5.2 > Playing the Game > Exploration > Vision and Light > Bright Light

title: Dim Light
summary: Dim light lightly obscures an area, making sight-based Perception less reliable.
body: Dim light is shadowy illumination between bright light and darkness. It commonly imposes disadvantage on Wisdom (Perception) checks that rely on sight.
tableUse: Use when characters are at torch edges, moonlight, smoke haze, or deep shadow.
commonCalls: Darkvision often treats darkness as dim light within range.
srdSection: SRD 5.2 > Playing the Game > Exploration > Vision and Light > Dim Light
srdText: Dim Light. Dim Light, also called shadows, creates a Lightly Obscured area. An area of Dim Light is usually a boundary between Bright Light and surrounding Darkness. The soft light of twilight and dawn also counts as Dim Light. A full moon might bathe the land in Dim Light.
full_text: Dim Light. Dim Light, also called shadows, creates a Lightly Obscured area. An area of Dim Light is usually a boundary between Bright Light and surrounding Darkness. The soft light of twilight and dawn also counts as Dim Light. A full moon might bathe the land in Dim Light.
page_ref: SRD 5.2 > Playing the Game > Exploration > Vision and Light > Dim Light

title: Darkness
summary: Darkness heavily obscures an area for creatures that cannot see through it.
body: A creature effectively cannot see into a heavily obscured area with normal sight. This can enable hiding and interfere with targeting or perception.
tableUse: Use when no meaningful light reaches the area or magical darkness applies.
commonCalls: Darkvision changes darkness to dim light within its range unless magical darkness says otherwise.
Heavily obscured is important for 2024 Hide gates.
srdSection: SRD 5.2 > Playing the Game > Exploration > Vision and Light > Darkness
srdText: Darkness. Darkness creates a Heavily Obscured area. Characters face Darkness outdoors at night (even most moonlit nights), within the confines of an unlit dungeon, or in an area of magical Darkness.
full_text: Darkness. Darkness creates a Heavily Obscured area. Characters face Darkness outdoors at night (even most moonlit nights), within the confines of an unlit dungeon, or in an area of magical Darkness.
page_ref: SRD 5.2 > Playing the Game > Exploration > Vision and Light > Darkness

title: Darkvision
summary: Darkvision lets a creature see in darkness within a listed range as if it were dim light.
body: Darkvision does not make darkness bright, and it usually does not reveal color in darkness. It helps with navigation but can still leave sight-based Perception impaired.
tableUse: Remember that darkvision has a range and does not eliminate every darkness penalty.
commonCalls: Dim light remains dim light; darkvision does not improve it to bright unless a rule says so.
srdText: Darkvision If you have Darkvision, you can see in Dim Light within a specified range as if it were Bright Light and in Darkness within that range as if it were Dim Light. You discern colors in that Darkness only as shades of gray. See also “Playing the Game” (“Exploration”).
full_text: Darkvision If you have Darkvision, you can see in Dim Light within a specified range as if it were Bright Light and in Darkness within that range as if it were Dim Light. You discern colors in that Darkness only as shades of gray. See also “Playing the Game” (“Exploration”).

title: Blindsight
summary: Blindsight lets a creature perceive nearby surroundings without relying on normal sight.
body: Within its listed range, a creature with blindsight can perceive things without sight, often bypassing darkness, invisibility, or blindness depending on the exact situation.
tableUse: Use the range strictly and decide whether barriers, silence, or special circumstances interfere.
commonCalls: Blindsight is not unlimited awareness.
It often counters invisible creatures within range.
srdText: Blindsight If you have Blindsight, you can see within a specific range without relying on physical sight. Within that range, you can see anything that isn’t behind Total Cover even if you have the Blinded condition or are in Darkness. Moreover, in that range, you can see something that has the Invisible condition.
full_text: Blindsight If you have Blindsight, you can see within a specific range without relying on physical sight. Within that range, you can see anything that isn’t behind Total Cover even if you have the Blinded condition or are in Darkness. Moreover, in that range, you can see something that has the Invisible condition.

title: Truesight
summary: Truesight is a powerful sense that sees through many visual deceptions within range.
body: A creature with truesight can usually see in normal and magical darkness, see invisible creatures, detect visual illusions, and perceive shapechanged or transformed forms within range.
tableUse: Use to neutralize many stealth and illusion tricks, but still respect range and nonvisual concealment.
commonCalls: Truesight does not automatically reveal motives or hidden information unrelated to perception.
srdText: Truesight If you have Truesight, your vision is enhanced within a specified range. Within that range, your vision pierces through the following: Darkness. You can see in normal and magical Darkness. Invisibility. You see creatures and objects that have the Invisible condition. Visual Illusions. Visual illusions appear transparent to you, and you automatically succeed on saving throws against them. Transformations. You discern the true form of any creature or object you see that has been transformed by magic. Ethereal Plane. You see into the Ethereal Plane.
full_text: Truesight If you have Truesight, your vision is enhanced within a specified range. Within that range, your vision pierces through the following: Darkness. You can see in normal and magical Darkness. Invisibility. You see creatures and objects that have the Invisible condition. Visual Illusions. Visual illusions appear transparent to you, and you automatically succeed on saving throws against them. Transformations. You discern the true form of any creature or object you see that has been transformed by magic. Ethereal Plane. You see into the Ethereal Plane.

title: Hiding and Stealth
summary: Hiding depends on cover, obscurity, noise, line of sight, and the observer's ability to find the hidden creature.
body: 2014 gives the DM broad authority over when hiding is possible. 2024 gives clearer gates through heavy obscurity or strong cover, a DC 15 Stealth check, and Invisible while hidden.
tableUse: Decide whether the creature can attempt to hide before asking for a roll.
commonCalls: Hidden does not always mean the enemy has no idea where you are.
Attacking or making loud noise often ends hiding.
srdSection: SRD 5.2 > Playing the Game > Exploration > Hiding; Rules Glossary > Hide [Action]
srdText:
Hiding Adventurers and monsters often hide, whether to spy on one another, sneak past a guardian, or set an ambush. The Game Master decides when circumstances are appropriate for hiding. When you try to hide, you take the Hide action.

Hide [Action] With the Hide action, you try to hide yourself. To do so, you must succeed on a DC 15 Dexterity (Stealth) check while you’re Heavily Obscured or behind Three-Quarters Cover or Total Cover, and you must be out of any enemy’s line of sight; if you can see a creature, you can discern whether it can see you. On a successful check, you have the Invisible condition while hidden. Make note of your check’s total, which is the DC for a creature to find you with a Wisdom (Perception) check. You stop being hidden immediately after any of the following occurs: you make a sound louder than a whisper, an enemy finds you, you make an attack roll, or you cast a spell with a Verbal component.
full_text:
Hiding Adventurers and monsters often hide, whether to spy on one another, sneak past a guardian, or set an ambush. The Game Master decides when circumstances are appropriate for hiding. When you try to hide, you take the Hide action.

Hide [Action] With the Hide action, you try to hide yourself. To do so, you must succeed on a DC 15 Dexterity (Stealth) check while you’re Heavily Obscured or behind Three-Quarters Cover or Total Cover, and you must be out of any enemy’s line of sight; if you can see a creature, you can discern whether it can see you. On a successful check, you have the Invisible condition while hidden. Make note of your check’s total, which is the DC for a creature to find you with a Wisdom (Perception) check. You stop being hidden immediately after any of the following occurs: you make a sound louder than a whisper, an enemy finds you, you make an attack roll, or you cast a spell with a Verbal component.
page_ref: SRD 5.2 > Playing the Game > Exploration > Hiding; Rules Glossary > Hide [Action]

title: Passive Checks
summary: A passive check represents an average result, usually 10 plus the relevant modifiers.
body: Use passive checks when the DM wants to determine what a creature notices or knows without calling for an active roll, or to represent repeated effort over time.
tableUse: Use passive Perception for hidden creatures, traps, ambushes, and clues that might be noticed automatically.
commonCalls: Advantage can be treated as +5 and disadvantage as -5 when using passive checks.
srdText:
Passive Perception
Passive Perception is a score that reflects a creature’s general awareness of its surroundings. The 
GM uses this score when determining whether a 
creature notices something without consciously 
making a Wisdom (Perception) check.
 A creature’s Passive Perception equals 10 plus the 
creature’s Wisdom (Perception) check bonus. If the 
creature has Advantage on such checks, increase 
the score by 5. If the creature has Disadvantage on 
them, decrease the score by 5. For example, a level 
1 character with a Wisdom of 15 and proficiency in 
Perception has a Passive Perception of 14 (10 + 2 + 
2). If that character has Advantage on Wisdom (Perception) checks, the score becomes 19.

2). If that character has Advantage on Wisdom (Perception) checks, the score becomes 19.

title: Armor Proficiency
summary: A creature can wear armor without proficiency, but doing so imposes serious penalties.
body: Armor proficiency represents training in moving and fighting while armored. Without proficiency, a character has disadvantage on ability checks, saving throws, and attack rolls that use Strength or Dexterity and cannot cast spells under the standard rules.
tableUse: Check armor proficiency whenever a character equips unfamiliar armor or a disguise includes armor.
commonCalls: Shields require proficiency separately from armor.
Class features, species traits, feats, and multiclassing can grant armor training.
srdText: Armor Training Armor training allows you to use armor of a certain category without the following drawbacks. If you wear Light, Medium, or Heavy armor and lack training with it, you have Disadvantage on any D20 Test that involves Strength or Dexterity, and you can’t cast spells. If you use a Shield and lack training with it, you don’t gain its AC bonus. See also “Disadvantage” and “Equipment” (“Armor”).
full_text: Armor Training Armor training allows you to use armor of a certain category without the following drawbacks. If you wear Light, Medium, or Heavy armor and lack training with it, you have Disadvantage on any D20 Test that involves Strength or Dexterity, and you can’t cast spells. If you use a Shield and lack training with it, you don’t gain its AC bonus. See also “Disadvantage” and “Equipment” (“Armor”).

title: Weapon Properties
summary: Weapon properties define how a weapon behaves beyond its damage die.
body: Properties such as Light, Finesse, Heavy, Reach, Thrown, Two-Handed, Versatile, Loading, and Ammunition change how the weapon is used. Always read the property and weapon table together.
tableUse: Use weapon properties when adjudicating dual wielding, thrown attacks, reach, ammunition, and special handling limits.
commonCalls: Finesse lets the attacker choose Strength or Dexterity.
Reach changes the distance at which melee attacks and opportunity attacks can occur.
srdText:
Properties
Here are definitions of the properties in the Properties column of the Weapons table.
Ammunition
You can use a weapon that has the Ammunition 
property to make a ranged attack only if you have 
ammunition to fire from it. The type of ammunition 
required is specified with the weapon’s range. Each 
attack expends one piece of ammunition. Drawing 
the ammunition is part of the attack (you need a 
free hand to load a one-handed weapon). After a 
fight, you can spend 1 minute to recover half the 
ammunition (round down) you used in the fight; the 
rest is lost.
Finesse
When making an attack with a Finesse weapon, use 
your choice of your Strength or Dexterity modifier 
for the attack and damage rolls. You must use the 
same modifier for both rolls.
Heavy
You have Disadvantage on attack rolls with a Heavy 
weapon if it’s a Melee weapon and your Strength 
score isn’t at least 13 or if it’s a Ranged weapon and 
your Dexterity score isn’t at least 13.
Light
When you take the Attack action on your turn and 
attack with a Light weapon, you can make one extra 
attack as a Bonus Action later on the same turn. 
That extra attack must be made with a different 
Light weapon, and you don’t add your ability modifier to the extra attack’s damage unless that modifier is negative. For example, you can attack with a 
Shortsword in one hand and a Dagger in the other 
using the Attack action and a Bonus Action, but you 
don’t add your Strength or Dexterity modifier to the 
damage roll of the Bonus Action unless that modifier is negative.

iMprovis Ed w Eapons
If you use an object—such as a table leg, frying pan, 
or bottle—as a makeshift weapon, see “Improvised 
Weapons” in “Rules Glossary.” Also see those rules 
if you wield a weapon in an unusual way, such as using 
a Ranged weapon to make a melee attack.
Loading
You can fire only one piece of ammunition from a 
Loading weapon when you use an action, a Bonus 
Action, or a Reaction to fire it, regardless of the 
number of attacks you can normally make.
Range
A Range weapon has a range in parentheses after 
the Ammunition or Thrown property. The range 
lists two numbers. The first is the weapon’s normal 
range in feet, and the second is the weapon’s long 
range. When attacking a target beyond normal 
range, you have Disadvantage on the attack roll. You 
can’t attack a target beyond the long range.
Reach
A Reach weapon adds 5 feet to your reach when you 
attack with it, as well as when determining your 
reach for Opportunity Attacks with it.
Thrown
If a weapon has the Thrown property, you can 
throw the weapon to make a ranged attack, and 
you can draw that weapon as part of the attack. If 
the weapon is a Melee weapon, use the same ability 
modifier for the attack and damage rolls that you 
use for a melee attack with that weapon.
Two-Handed
A Two-Handed weapon requires two hands when 
you attack with it.
Versatile
A Versatile weapon can be used with one or two 
hands. A damage value in parentheses appears with 
the property. The weapon deals that damage when 
used with two hands to make a melee attack.

title: Carrying Capacity and Encumbrance
summary: Carrying capacity determines how much a character can reasonably carry; variant encumbrance adds speed penalties.
body: The default carrying capacity is based on Strength and size. Some tables use variant encumbrance to make heavy loads reduce Speed and impose penalties. The exact variant is optional and should be chosen before inventory pressure matters.
tableUse: Use when treasure, armor, survival gear, or mounts make weight a meaningful constraint.
commonCalls: Tiny or larger creatures modify carrying capacity by size.
Containers do not erase weight unless magical.
srdText:
Carrying Capacity
Your size and Strength score determine the maximum weight in pounds that you can carry, as shown 
in the Carrying Capacity table. The table also shows 
the maximum weight you can drag, lift, or push.
 While dragging, lifting, or pushing weight in excess of the maximum weight you can carry, your 
Speed can be no more than 5 feet.
Carrying Capacity
Creature Size Carry Drag/Lift/Push
Tiny Str. × 7.5 lb. Str. × 15 lb.
Small/Medium Str. × 15 lb. Str. × 30 lb.
Large Str. × 30 lb. Str. × 60 lb.
Huge Str. × 60 lb. Str. × 120 lb.
Gargantuan Str. × 120 lb. Str. × 240 lb.
full_text:

Speed can be no more than 5 feet.

title: Weapon Mastery
summary: Weapon Mastery lets trained characters use a weapon's mastery property when they attack with that weapon.
body: Each 2024 weapon has a mastery property. A character needs a feature, feat, or other rule that grants mastery with that weapon, and usually proficiency with it, to use that property. Masteries add tactical effects such as pushing, slowing, nicking, cleaving, grazing, vexing, or toppling.
tableUse: Check the character's mastered weapons and the weapon property before applying a mastery effect.
commonCalls: Nick changes two-weapon action economy.
Topple and similar effects often require saves or limited triggers.
srdText:
Mastery Properties
Each weapon has a mastery property, which is usable only by a character who has a feature, such as 
Weapon Mastery, that unlocks the property for the 
character. The properties are defined below.
Cleave
If you hit a creature with a melee attack roll using 
this weapon, you can make a melee attack roll with 
the weapon against a second creature within 5 feet 
of the first that is also within your reach. On a hit, 
the second creature takes the weapon’s damage, 
but don’t add your ability modifier to that damage 
unless that modifier is negative. You can make this 
extra attack only once per turn.
Graze
If your attack roll with this weapon misses a creature, you can deal damage to that creature equal 
to the ability modifier you used to make the attack 
roll. This damage is the same type dealt by the 
weapon, and the damage can be increased only by 
increasing the ability modifier.
Nick
When you make the extra attack of the Light property, you can make it as part of the Attack action instead of as a Bonus Action. You can make this extra 
attack only once per turn.
Push
If you hit a creature with this weapon, you can push 
the creature up to 10 feet straight away from yourself if it is Large or smaller.
Sap
If you hit a creature with this weapon, that creature 
has Disadvantage on its next attack roll before the 
start of your next turn.
Slow
If you hit a creature with this weapon and deal 
damage to it, you can reduce its Speed by 10 feet until the start of your next turn. If the creature is hit 
more than once by weapons that have this property, 
the Speed reduction doesn’t exceed 10 feet.
Topple
If you hit a creature with this weapon, you can force 
the creature to make a Constitution saving throw 
(DC 8 plus the ability modifier used to make the 
attack roll and your Proficiency Bonus). On a failed 
save, the creature has the Prone condition.
Vex
If you hit a creature with this weapon and deal damage to the creature, you have Advantage on your 
next attack roll against that creature before the end 
of your next turn.

title: Magic Item Rarity
summary: Magic items have six rarity tiers - Common, Uncommon, Rare, Very Rare, Legendary, and Artifact - used to gauge power, availability, and approximate value.
body:
Rarity provides a rough measure of an item's power relative to other magic items. Rarity also indicates the item's availability in the world.

| Rarity | Character Level | Approx. Value |
|--------|----------------|---------------|
| Common | 1st or higher | 50-100 gp |
| Uncommon | 1st or higher | 101-500 gp |
| Rare | 5th or higher | 501-5,000 gp |
| Very Rare | 11th or higher | 5,001-50,000 gp |
| Legendary | 17th or higher | 50,001+ gp |
| Artifact | Special | Unique / Priceless |

These values are guidelines for trading or selling items, not fixed prices. Actual value depends heavily on your campaign world and how available magic items are.

Minor magic items (Common, Uncommon) are more widely available. Major items (Rare, Very Rare, Legendary) are rare finds or quest rewards. Artifacts are unique objects of immense power tied to the history of the world.
tableUse: Use rarity to quickly gauge whether an item is appropriate for the party's level and to set expectations when they try to buy or sell magic items.
commonCalls: Rarity does not strictly determine whether a party can use an item - only whether they can attune to it (some items require attunement) and how powerful it is.
The gold values are rough estimates. Many campaigns treat magic items as not for sale at all, making rarity primarily a power gauge.
Artifacts are not assigned a rarity tier in the normal sense - they are unique objects with their own rules and properties.
In 2024, the same rarity tiers exist with the same names and general level guidance.
dmImpact: Use the character level column as a soft guideline for when to introduce items of each tier. A party of 5th-level characters finding a Legendary item is unusual and should feel like a major story event.
dmSummary: Magic items have six rarity tiers - Common, Uncommon, Rare, Very Rare, Legendary, and Artifact - used to gauge power, availability, and approximate value.

| Artifact | Special | Unique / Priceless |

title: Magic Item Attunement
summary: A creature can normally attune to no more than three magic items at a time.
body: Attunement usually requires a short rest spent focused on the item while in physical contact with it. Attunement can end if prerequisites fail, the creature dies, another creature attunes, distance persists, or the creature spends a short rest ending it unless cursed.
tableUse: Track attunement slots when awarding persistent magic items with combat or utility power.
commonCalls: A fourth attunement attempt fails until one attunement ends.
You cannot usually attune to more than one copy of the same item.
srdText: Attunement Some magic items require a creature to form a bond with them before their magical properties can be used. This bond is called attunement, and certain items have a prerequisite for it. If the prerequisite is a class, a creature must be a member of that class to attune to the item. (If the class is a spellcasting class, a monster qualifies if it has spell slots and uses that class’s spell list.) If the prerequisite is to be a spellcaster, a creature qualifies if it can cast at least one spell using its traits or features, not using a magic item or the like. Without becoming attuned to an item that requires attunement, a creature gains only its nonmagical benefits, unless its description states otherwise. For example, a magic shield that requires attunement provides the benefits of a normal shield to a creature not attuned to it, but none of its magical properties. Attuning to an item requires a creature to spend a short rest focused on only that item while being in physical contact with it (this can’t be the same short rest used to learn the item’s properties). This focus can take the form of weapon practice (for a weapon), meditation (for a wondrous item), or some other appropriate activity. If the short rest is interrupted, the attunement attempt fails. Otherwise, at the end of the short rest, the creature gains an intuitive understanding of how to activate any magical properties of the item, including any necessary command words. An item can be attuned to only one creature at a time, and a creature can be attuned to no more than three magic items at a time. Any attempt to attune to a fourth item fails; the creature must end its attunement to an item first. Additionally, a creature can’t attune to more than one copy of an item. For example, a creature can’t attune to more than one ring of protection at a time. A creature’s attunement to an item ends if the creature no longer satisfies the prerequisites for attunement, if the item has been more than 100 feet away for at least 24 hours, if the creature dies, or if another creature attunes to the item. A creature can also voluntarily end attunement by spending another short rest focused on the item, unless the item is cursed.
full_text: Attunement Some magic items require a creature to form a bond with them before their magical properties can be used. This bond is called attunement, and certain items have a prerequisite for it. If the prerequisite is a class, a creature must be a member of that class to attune to the item. (If the class is a spellcasting class, a monster qualifies if it has spell slots and uses that class’s spell list.) If the prerequisite is to be a spellcaster, a creature qualifies if it can cast at least one spell using its traits or features, not using a magic item or the like. Without becoming attuned to an item that requires attunement, a creature gains only its nonmagical benefits, unless its description states otherwise. For example, a magic shield that requires attunement provides the benefits of a normal shield to a creature not attuned to it, but none of its magical properties. Attuning to an item requires a creature to spend a short rest focused on only that item while being in physical contact with it (this can’t be the same short rest used to learn the item’s properties). This focus can take the form of weapon practice (for a weapon), meditation (for a wondrous item), or some other appropriate activity. If the short rest is interrupted, the attunement attempt fails. Otherwise, at the end of the short rest, the creature gains an intuitive understanding of how to activate any magical properties of the item, including any necessary command words. An item can be attuned to only one creature at a time, and a creature can be attuned to no more than three magic items at a time. Any attempt to attune to a fourth item fails; the creature must end its attunement to an item first. Additionally, a creature can’t attune to more than one copy of an item. For example, a creature can’t attune to more than one ring of protection at a time. A creature’s attunement to an item ends if the creature no longer satisfies the prerequisites for attunement, if the item has been more than 100 feet away for at least 24 hours, if the creature dies, or if another creature attunes to the item. A creature can also voluntarily end attunement by spending another short rest focused on the item, unless the item is cursed.

title: Identifying Magic Items
summary: A creature can identify a magic item's properties by casting Identify, by short rest experimentation, or by attuning to it. Some items conceal their nature.
body:
There are three main ways to identify a magic item:

**Identify Spell.** Casting Identify on a magic item immediately reveals its properties, whether it requires attunement, and how many charges it has. It also reveals spells currently affecting the item.

**Short Rest Experimentation.** A creature that handles an item during a short rest can focus on it. At the end of the rest, the creature learns the item's properties and how to use them, whether it requires attunement, and how many charges it has. Some items are designed to be difficult to identify this way - curses, for example, are typically not revealed until the item is used.

**Attunement.** Spending a short rest attuning to an item reveals its properties as part of the attunement process.

**Cursed Items.** The Identify spell does not reveal that an item is cursed. A curse typically remains hidden until the character actually uses the item or until Remove Curse or similar magic is employed.

**Identifying by DM Description.** Some properties are self-evident from how the item behaves (a sword that glows when undead are near, for example).
tableUse: Use this when players find a magic item and ask 'what does it do?' Set the pace of revelation based on your campaign style - instant Identify is valid, deliberate experimentation builds mystery.
commonCalls: The Identify spell does NOT reveal curses. Players who rely on Identify alone may be surprised by a cursed item's effects.
Short rest experimentation requires the creature to be in contact with the item and focused on it for the entire rest.
Items that require attunement typically don't reveal all their properties until attuned.
Charges remaining and recharge conditions are revealed by Identify or short rest focus.
Multiple items can be identified during the same short rest if the creature focuses on each one.
dmImpact: Consider whether you want your world to have easy access to Identify (makes items feel like equipment) or limit it (makes items feel mysterious and dangerous). Both are valid campaign styles.
dmSummary: A creature can identify a magic item's properties by casting Identify, by short rest experimentation, or by attuning to it. Some items conceal their nature.

title: Downtime Activities
summary: Between adventures, characters can engage in downtime activities - extended tasks that take days or weeks and provide meaningful rewards.
body:
Downtime activities are tasks characters undertake between adventures, representing the passage of time and giving players agency over their characters' lives outside of dungeon-crawling.

One downtime day = 8 hours of activity. Most activities are measured in workweeks (5 days) or longer.

**Core 2014 DMG Downtime Activities:**

- **Crafting.** Create weapons, armor, or other items. Cost is half the item's market value; progress is 5 gp of value per day.
- **Practicing a Profession.** Work a job to maintain a modest lifestyle for free, or earn modest income (1d10 sp per day above the lifestyle cost).
- **Recuperating.** 3 days of rest removes one level of Exhaustion or ends one disease or poison effect.
- **Research.** Spend 1 gp/day plus access to a library or informant; the DM provides lore or answers about a specific topic.
- **Training.** Spend 250 days and 1 gp/day to learn a new language or gain proficiency with a tool. The DM may allow accelerated training with good roleplay.

**Xanathar's Guide Extended Activities** include buying a magic item, carousing, crafting a magic item, crime, gambling, pit fighting, relaxation, religious service, researching, scribing scrolls, selling a magic item, training to gain levels, and work.

**2024 DMG Updates:** The 2024 DMG retains the concept of downtime but restructures several activities with DC-based outcome tables rather than fixed mechanical results.
tableUse: Use at the start of a session when significant time has passed in-world. Ask each player what their character did during downtime. Most activities require only a sentence of resolution.
commonCalls: One workweek = 5 workdays = 40 hours of activity. Characters can maintain a lifestyle during downtime.
Downtime progress is interrupted if the character goes on an adventure - partial progress is typically paused, not lost.
The DM decides how much time passes between sessions. Not every gap needs downtime resolution.
Characters can pursue multiple activities sequentially but not simultaneously during the same workweek.
Training takes 250 days total - encourage players to track this across many sessions.
dmImpact: Downtime rewards players who engage with the world between adventures. Even a brief 'you spend the week doing X' exchange makes the world feel real.
dmSummary: Between adventures, characters can engage in downtime activities - extended tasks that take days or weeks and provide meaningful rewards.

title: Crafting Items
summary: Characters can craft nonmagical gear, potions, and spell scrolls during downtime given the right proficiencies, tools, and materials.
body:
**Crafting Nonmagical Items (DMG 2014)**
A character must have proficiency with the tools relevant to the item. Progress is made at a rate of 5 gp of item value per workday. Multiple characters can contribute to a single item - combine their daily progress.

**Crafting Potions of Healing (Xanathar's)**
| Potion | Cost | Time |
|--------|------|------|
| Healing | 25 gp | 1 day |
| Greater Healing | 100 gp | 1 workweek |
| Superior Healing | 1,000 gp | 3 workweeks |
| Supreme Healing | 10,000 gp | 4 workweeks |

**Scribing Spell Scrolls (Xanathar's)**
| Spell Level | Cost | Time | Min. Character Level |
|-------------|------|------|---------------------|
| Cantrip | 15 gp | 1 day | 1st |
| 1st | 25 gp | 1 day | 1st |
| 2nd | 250 gp | 3 days | 3rd |
| 3rd | 500 gp | 1 workweek | 5th |
| 4th | 2,500 gp | 2 workweeks | 7th |
| 5th | 5,000 gp | 4 workweeks | 9th |
| 6th | 15,000 gp | 8 workweeks | 11th |
| 7th | 25,000 gp | 16 workweeks | 13th |
| 8th | 50,000 gp | 20 workweeks | 15th |
| 9th | 250,000 gp | 48 workweeks | 17th |

**Crafting Magic Items (Xanathar's)** requires the Spellcasting feature, relevant tool proficiency, a formula, and significant time/cost investment.
tableUse: Use when a player says 'I want to craft X between sessions.' Look up the relevant category and tell them how long it takes and what it costs.
commonCalls: A character can only scribe scrolls for spells they have prepared or know.
Multiple proficient characters can combine progress on a nonmagical item.
Crafting progress pauses but does not reset if the character goes on an adventure.
The DM decides whether formulas are required for magic items.
A character must expend a spell slot of the appropriate level each time they scribe a spell scroll.
dmImpact: Crafting scrolls and potions gives non-combat characters a meaningful downtime loop. Be consistent with the costs to avoid friction.
dmSummary: Characters can craft nonmagical gear, potions, and spell scrolls during downtime given the right proficiencies, tools, and materials.

title: Social Interaction
summary: Social encounters follow a three-step structure: establish NPC attitude, engage through roleplay, then call for an ability check if the outcome is uncertain.
body:
**NPC Attitudes**
Every NPC in a social situation has an attitude that shapes how they respond:

- **Friendly** - Helpful and cooperative.
- **Indifferent** - Neutral. Acts in their own self-interest.
- **Hostile** - Opposed. May attack, refuse, or work against the party.

**Social Interaction Procedure**
1. Roleplay first.
2. Determine if a roll is needed.
3. Set the DC based on the request and the NPC's attitude.
4. Choose the skill: Persuasion, Deception, or Intimidation.
5. Apply the outcome.

**2024 Addition:** The 2024 PHB adds the Influence action as a formal combat/interaction action, giving social skills a defined action-economy context during structured encounters.
tableUse: Use this procedure whenever players enter a meaningful social scene with an NPC. It works for negotiations, interrogations, court encounters, and persuading reluctant allies.
commonCalls: Don't skip the roleplay. A player who makes a great argument should have a real advantage.
Charisma skills affect NPC attitudes, not NPC beliefs.
Intimidation can get short-term compliance but often worsens long-term relations.
Deception vs. Insight is an opposed check when an NPC actively suspects a lie.
Some NPCs are simply not persuadable on certain topics.
dmImpact: The best social encounters have stakes before the dice come out. Know what the NPC wants, what they fear, and what would change their mind.
dmSummary: Social encounters follow a three-step structure: establish NPC attitude, engage through roleplay, then call for an ability check if the outcome is uncertain.

title: Extreme Heat and Cold
summary: Extreme temperatures force Constitution saving throws each hour; failure inflicts Exhaustion. Proper gear negates the risk.
body:
**Extreme Cold**
Temperatures at or below 0°F (-18°C). Without cold weather gear, a creature must succeed on a DC 10 Constitution saving throw at the end of each hour or gain one level of Exhaustion. Creatures with resistance or immunity to cold damage automatically succeed.

**Extreme Heat**
Temperatures at or above 100°F (38°C) in direct sunlight, or equivalent environments. A creature without access to water and in medium or heavy armor (or who naturally has a thick coat of fur) must make a Constitution saving throw at the end of each hour. The DC starts at 5 and increases by 1 for each hour. On a failure, the creature gains one level of Exhaustion. Creatures with resistance or immunity to fire damage automatically succeed.

Water prevents the Constitution saving throw from being required against extreme heat.
tableUse: Use when the party travels through arctic tundra, volcanic regions, deserts, or similar environments. Roll at the end of each hour of travel or exposure.
commonCalls: Heat only requires a save if the creature lacks water access.
Cold weather gear negates extreme cold entirely.
Multiple hours of failure stack Exhaustion levels quickly.
Fire and cold resistance handle the relevant temperature.
Use Exhaustion carefully - it compounds rapidly.
dmImpact: Environment as a hazard works best when the party knows it's coming and has to prepare.
dmSummary: Extreme temperatures force Constitution saving throws each hour; failure inflicts Exhaustion. Proper gear negates the risk.

title: Wind and Precipitation
summary: Strong winds impose disadvantage on ranged attacks and Perception checks; heavy precipitation lightly obscures areas and hampers hearing.
body:
**Strong Wind**
A strong wind imposes disadvantage on ranged weapon attack rolls and Wisdom (Perception) checks that rely on hearing. It extinguishes open flames, disperses fog, and makes nonmagical flight difficult or impossible.

**Heavy Precipitation**
Heavy rain, sleet, or snow lightly obscures the area. Creatures in a lightly obscured area have disadvantage on Wisdom (Perception) checks that rely on sight. Heavy precipitation also extinguishes open flames and imposes disadvantage on Wisdom (Perception) checks that rely on hearing.

**Fog**
Dense natural fog creates a heavily obscured area. Creatures in a heavily obscured area are effectively Blinded.
tableUse: Apply these effects when the party is in adverse weather. For travel, they may combine with travel pace and Exhaustion saves.
commonCalls: Strong wind affects ranged weapon attacks, not necessarily ranged spell attacks.
Lightly obscured means disadvantage on sight-based Perception regardless of darkvision.
Strong wind extinguishing a torch or campfire is usually just flavor.
Magical flight can still work, though the DM may call for checks to maintain control.
dmImpact: Weather makes travel feel real and threatening without needing to be deadly. Apply the mechanics sparingly for effect.
dmSummary: Strong winds impose disadvantage on ranged attacks and Perception checks; heavy precipitation lightly obscures areas and hampers hearing.

title: Traps
summary: Traps have a trigger, an effect, and a DC to detect and disarm. Detecting uses Perception or Investigation; disarming usually requires Thieves' Tools or a relevant ability check.
body:
**Trap Elements**
Every trap has three components:
1. **Trigger** - The condition that activates the trap.
2. **Effect** - What happens when triggered.
3. **Countermeasures** - How the trap can be detected, avoided, or disabled.

**Detecting a Trap**
Passive Perception can reveal suspicious signs. Active Investigation can locate or understand trap mechanisms.

**Disabling a Trap**
Mechanical traps typically require a Dexterity check with Thieves' Tools. Magical traps usually require Dispel Magic, a specific countermeasure, or an Arcana/Intelligence check.

**Trap Severity**
| Severity | Save DC | Attack Bonus | Damage |
|----------|---------|--------------|--------|
| Setback | 10-11 | +3 to +5 | 1d10 |
| Dangerous | 12-15 | +6 to +8 | 4d10 |
| Deadly | 16-20 | +9 to +12 | 10d10 |
tableUse: Place traps where they make narrative sense - guarding valuables, protecting a villain's sanctum, or left by a previous occupant.
commonCalls: Always tell players what they detect, not whether it is a trap.
A character who fails to detect a trap and triggers it should have had a chance to discover the danger.
Thieves' Tools proficiency is required to add proficiency bonus to the disarm check.
Magical traps are not usually solved by Thieves' Tools.
A trap that kills on a failed save is almost always too severe.
dmImpact: Traps reward careful play. Start with memorable, survivable traps before using lethal ones.
dmSummary: Traps have a trigger, an effect, and a DC to detect and disarm. Detecting uses Perception or Investigation; disarming usually requires Thieves' Tools or a relevant ability check.

title: Diseases and Poisons
summary: Diseases and poisons impose ongoing effects requiring Constitution saves to resist or recover from. They come in several types with different delivery methods.
body:
**Poison Types and Delivery**
| Type | How Delivered | Example |
|------|--------------|--------|
| Contact | Touching the substance | Poison on a surface |
| Ingested | Eating or drinking | Poisoned wine |
| Inhaled | Breathing gas or powder | Toxic fumes |
| Injury | Wound from a poisoned weapon/bite | Serpent venom |

**Poisoned Condition**
A creature that is poisoned has disadvantage on attack rolls and ability checks. Many poison effects also deal damage or impose additional conditions.

**Diseases**
Diseases are ongoing hazards from environments or infected creatures. A creature exposed usually makes Constitution saves at fixed intervals. Successes may end the disease; failures may progress it.

**Ending Effects**
Lesser Restoration, Neutralize Poison, Greater Restoration, Lay on Hands, or natural recovery can end certain poison or disease effects depending on the source.
tableUse: Apply when a creature is bitten by a venomous monster, eats tainted food, or is in a diseased environment. Call for Constitution saves; track duration and progress carefully.
commonCalls: Check the monster stat block for the specific poison DC and effect.
Poison damage is separate from the Poisoned condition.
Disease duration is typically measured in days.
Antitoxin grants advantage on Constitution saves against poison for 1 hour.
A Paladin's Lay on Hands can cure one disease or one poison effect.
dmImpact: Diseases work best as long-term environmental threats, not instant death. Let the party feel the clock while giving them a real cure path.
dmSummary: Diseases and poisons impose ongoing effects requiring Constitution saves to resist or recover from. They come in several types with different delivery methods.
nonSrdText:
**Poison Types and Delivery**
| Type | How Delivered | Example |
|------|--------------|--------|
| Contact | Touching the substance | Poison on a surface |
| Ingested | Eating or drinking | Poisoned wine |
| Inhaled | Breathing gas or powder | Toxic fumes |
| Injury | Wound from a poisoned weapon/bite | Serpent venom |

**Poisoned Condition**
A creature that is poisoned has disadvantage on attack rolls and ability checks. Many poison effects also deal damage or impose additional conditions.

**Diseases**
Diseases are ongoing hazards from environments or infected creatures. A creature exposed usually makes Constitution saves at fixed intervals. Successes may end the disease; failures may progress it.

**Ending Effects**
Lesser Restoration, Neutralize Poison, Greater Restoration, Lay on Hands, or natural recovery can end certain poison or disease effects depending on the source.

title: Chases
summary: Chase rules abstract pursuit across terrain as a series of Dash actions and Complication checks, preventing chases from reducing to pure speed comparison.
body:
Chases handle situations where one group is pursuing another over longer distances than a standard combat round.

**Setting Up a Chase**
1. Determine the starting distance between pursuer and quarry.
2. The chase plays out in rounds, same as combat.

**Taking the Dash Action**
Every participant can take the Dash action in addition to their normal movement. However, a creature can Dash a limited number of times during a chase before risking Exhaustion from additional Dashes.

**Complications**
At the end of each round, participants roll on a Complication table appropriate to the environment. Complications add obstacles such as locked gates, crowds, slippery ground, or hazards.

**Escaping**
A quarry escapes if it breaks line of sight and successfully hides, or if it gets far enough away that pursuers cannot maintain contact.
tableUse: Initiate a chase when a creature flees and the distance prevents normal combat resolution. Use it for both party pursuit and party flight.
commonCalls: Track Exhaustion during long chases.
Complications can affect both quarry and pursuer.
Flying creatures can use chase rules with airborne hazards.
Do not let a faster creature automatically win; complications create drama.
A chase ends when the quarry escapes, is caught, or all participants stop.
dmImpact: The Complication table is what makes chases fun instead of pure speed math. Customize it to the environment.
dmSummary: Chase rules abstract pursuit across terrain as a series of Dash actions and Complication checks, preventing chases from reducing to pure speed comparison.

title: Vehicles
summary: Vehicles have speed, crew requirements, cargo capacity, and AC/HP. In combat they move on the pilot's initiative and follow simplified attack rules.
body:
**Vehicle Statistics**
Vehicles have speed, crew requirements, passenger capacity, cargo capacity, AC, HP, and sometimes a damage threshold.

**Common Vehicle Speeds**
| Vehicle | Speed (mph) | Miles/Day |
|---------|------------|----------|
| Rowboat | 1.5 | 36 |
| Keelboat | 1 | 24 |
| Sailing Ship | 2 | 48 |
| Warship | 2.5 | 60 |
| Galley | 4 | 96 |
| Longship | 3 | 72 |
| Wagon | 2.5 | 60 |

**Vehicles in Combat**
Vehicles generally use the pilot's initiative count. Crew use mounted weapons or their own actions. Damage threshold means small hits may not damage the vehicle at all.
tableUse: Use the speed table for overland and water travel planning. In vehicle combat, track HP and crew losses separately.
commonCalls: Damage threshold means small arms fire barely scratches most ships.
A vehicle without minimum crew cannot be piloted.
Boarding actions work normally once a creature climbs onto the vehicle.
For extended naval campaigns, consider expanded vehicle rules.
Simple wagon combat can be treated as terrain plus an object with AC/HP.
dmImpact: Vehicles add tactical geometry. Even a river barge can make a fight feel different.
dmSummary: Vehicles have speed, crew requirements, cargo capacity, and AC/HP. In combat they move on the pilot's initiative and follow simplified attack rules.

title: Encounter Building
summary: Build balanced encounters by totaling monster XP values, applying a multiplier for monster count, and comparing to the party's XP difficulty thresholds.
body:
**Step-by-Step Encounter Building (2014 DMG)**
1. Establish difficulty: Easy, Medium, Hard, or Deadly.
2. Determine the XP threshold by character level and party size.
3. Select monsters and note each monster's XP value.
4. Calculate adjusted XP: raw monster XP total multiplied by the Monster Number Multiplier.
5. Compare adjusted XP to the party threshold.

Adjusted XP is only for measuring difficulty; award raw XP after the fight.

**2024 DMG Approach**
The 2024 DMG revises encounter building with different budget guidance, but the practical goal remains the same: estimate danger before play and adjust for context.
tableUse: Use this whenever designing a combat encounter. It prevents accidental TPKs or trivially easy fights.
commonCalls: Deadly does not guarantee a TPK; it means the encounter is dangerous and resource-intensive.
Adjusted XP is for calibration only. Award raw XP.
The multiplier makes hordes much more dangerous.
Terrain matters more than the math.
The system works best for medium-sized parties.
dmImpact: Encounter math is a starting point, not a guarantee. Trust your feel for the party after a few sessions.
dmSummary: Build balanced encounters by totaling monster XP values, applying a multiplier for monster count, and comparing to the party's XP difficulty thresholds.

4. Calculate adjusted XP: raw monster XP total multiplied by the Monster Number Multiplier.
5. Compare adjusted XP to the party threshold.

title: Adventuring Day Budget
summary: The adventuring day budget estimates how much total XP worth of encounters a party can handle before needing a long rest.
body:
The adventuring day budget represents the cumulative XP value of all encounters before the party is forced to rest. It helps make limited resources matter.

**Adventuring Day XP Budget by Party Level**
| Level | XP/Day per Character |
|-------|---------------------|
| 1 | 300 |
| 2 | 600 |
| 3 | 1,200 |
| 4 | 1,700 |
| 5 | 3,500 |
| 6 | 4,000 |
| 7 | 5,000 |
| 8 | 6,000 |
| 9 | 7,500 |
| 10 | 9,000 |
| 11 | 10,500 |
| 12 | 11,500 |
| 13 | 13,500 |
| 14 | 15,000 |
| 15 | 18,000 |
| 16 | 20,000 |
| 17 | 25,000 |
| 18 | 27,000 |
| 19 | 30,000 |
| 20 | 40,000 |

A typical adventuring day in the 2014 DMG assumes several medium or hard encounters and short rests between some of them.
tableUse: Use this to plan a dungeon level or a day of hostile travel. If resource attrition should matter, plan several encounters before the next long rest.
commonCalls: The budget assumes roughly 2 short rests per day.
Not all encounters need to be combat.
Several medium fights often tax resources more than one hard fight.
If the party long-rests mid-dungeon, let the dungeon respond.
dmImpact: The adventuring day is a design tool, not a rule. Use it when limited resources should create tension.

title: Treasure Hoards
summary: Treasure hoards are rolled using CR-tiered tables that generate coins, gems, art objects, and magic items appropriate to the encounter's challenge level.
body:
Treasure hoards represent significant finds - the accumulated wealth of a villain, a dragon's lair, or a dungeon boss.

**Hoard Tiers by CR**
| CR Range | Roll Table |
|----------|----------|
| CR 0-4 | Hoard: Challenge 0-4 |
| CR 5-10 | Hoard: Challenge 5-10 |
| CR 11-16 | Hoard: Challenge 11-16 |
| CR 17+ | Hoard: Challenge 17+ |

Hoards can include coins, gems, art objects, and magic items. Individual monster treasure usually produces only coins. You are never required to roll randomly; hand-picking story-fitting items is always valid.
tableUse: Roll a hoard when the party defeats a major enemy or discovers a significant vault. Match the CR range to the encounter's difficulty.
commonCalls: The hoard tables generate treasure in the DMG; this card gives the framework.
Electrum pieces can be replaced if your table ignores them.
Magic item results can be rerolled if inappropriate.
You can split one hoard across multiple rooms.
dmImpact: Random hoards are fun. Hand-picked items build narrative. Combining both often works best.
dmSummary: Treasure hoards are rolled using CR-tiered tables that generate coins, gems, art objects, and magic items appropriate to the encounter's challenge level.

title: Identifying Treasure and Magic
summary: Characters can identify the nature and value of mundane treasure by examining it; magic items require Identify, attunement, or short-rest experimentation.
body:
**Mundane Treasure**
Coins are immediately recognizable. Gems and art objects can be appraised by examination, background knowledge, or a relevant check. The DM may simply tell players what they find when they examine it carefully.

**Common Gem Values**
| Value | Examples |
|-------|----------|
| 10 gp | Azurite, blue quartz, hematite, malachite |
| 50 gp | Bloodstone, carnelian, chalcedony, citrine |
| 100 gp | Amber, amethyst, coral, jade |
| 500 gp | Alexandrite, aquamarine, garnet |
| 1,000 gp | Opal, star ruby, star sapphire |
| 5,000 gp | Black sapphire, diamond, emerald, ruby |

**Magic Items**
Identification uses Identify, attunement, or short-rest experimentation. Cursed items often conceal their drawbacks.
tableUse: Use when the party finds gems or art objects and asks what they're worth. Pick from the gem table or assign a value based on description.
commonCalls: Electrum pieces are worth 5 sp each.
Art objects can carry story value beyond gold.
Players may negotiate for more than half value.
Gems are easier to transport than coins.
dmImpact: Treasure identification does not need mechanical fanfare. Save mystery for magic items.
dmSummary: Characters can identify the nature and value of mundane treasure by examining it; magic items require Identify, attunement, or short-rest experimentation.

title: Improvising Rules
summary: When the rules don't cover a situation, the DM makes a quick ruling: set a DC, pick an ability score, call for a save, and move on. Correct it later if needed.
body:
D&D cannot anticipate every situation players will create. The DM fills gaps quickly and consistently so play keeps moving.

**Core Framework**
1. Decide whether success is automatic or impossible.
2. Pick the most relevant ability check.
3. Set a DC using the standard difficulty scale.
4. Call for a saving throw if the character is avoiding danger.
5. Make the call and move on.

**Rulings, Not Rules**
If a player describes an action that makes narrative sense but has no mechanical framework, reward creativity with an appropriate check and a meaningful outcome.
tableUse: Use this mental process whenever a player tries something the rules do not explicitly cover.
commonCalls: 'There's no rule for that' should rarely be the final answer.
Tell the player what kind of check before they roll.
Let creative ability/skill pairings work when they fit.
Write down rulings you make mid-session for consistency.
dmImpact: Confidence is the ruling DM skill. Players remember whether the moment felt fair and dramatic more than whether the rule was perfect.
dmSummary: When the rules don't cover a situation, the DM makes a quick ruling: set a DC, pick an ability score, call for a save, and move on. Correct it later if needed.

title: Optional Rule: Flanking
summary: When two allies are on directly opposite sides of an enemy, both have advantage on melee attacks against it. This is an optional rule - not active by default.
body:
**Flanking (Optional Rule)**
A creature is flanked when two or more hostile creatures that can see it are on directly opposite sides of it.

When a creature is flanked:
- Both flanking creatures have advantage on melee attack rolls against the flanked creature.
- The flanking creatures must be able to see the target.
- The flanking creatures must be using melee weapons or unarmed strikes.

This is an optional rule. It is not active unless the table explicitly uses it.

**Recommended Alternative**
Instead of advantage, flanking can grant a flat +2 to melee attack rolls.
tableUse: Decide before session one whether flanking is in use at your table. Tell the players clearly.
commonCalls: Flanking only applies when creatures are on opposite sides.
Flanking does not apply to ranged attacks.
Large creatures are easier to flank.
Some creatures may be hard or impossible to flank at DM discretion.
changeSummary: The 2024 DMG does not include flanking as an optional rule. It was removed due to concerns about Advantage inflation. This rule is 2014 DMG only.
dmImpact: Flanking rewards positioning but can make advantage too common. Try it only if your table wants that tactical texture.
dmSummary: When two allies are on directly opposite sides of an enemy, both have advantage on melee attacks against it. This is an optional rule - not active by default.

title: Optional Rule: Hero Points
summary: Hero Points give characters a pool of points they can spend to add 1d6 to any attack roll, ability check, or saving throw after the roll.
body:
**Hero Points (Optional Rule)**
Hero Points give characters a limited resource to spend on critical moments.

**Earning Hero Points**
Characters start with a small pool and regain points when they gain a level. The DM may award points for exceptional moments.

**Spending Hero Points**
A character can spend one hero point on an attack roll, ability check, or saving throw after seeing the result but before the outcome is determined. Add 1d6 to the result. Only one hero point can be spent per roll.

**Comparison to 2024 Heroic Inspiration**
Heroic Inspiration in 2024 lets a character reroll one die rather than adding 1d6.
tableUse: Introduce this rule at session zero if you want a safety valve for critical moments. It is useful in lethal campaigns.
commonCalls: The key timing is after the roll but before success or failure is declared.
Only one hero point can be spent per roll.
Hero Points reset on level up, not on long rest.
Tell players their point total openly so they use them.
changeSummary: This optional rule predates the 2024 Heroic Inspiration system. Hero Points add to the roll; 2024 Heroic Inspiration rerolls a die.
dmImpact: Hero Points reward players for leaning into big moments. Make the reset condition clear so they spend them.
dmSummary: Hero Points give characters a pool of points they can spend to add 1d6 to any attack roll, ability check, or saving throw after the roll.

title: Bastions
summary: Bastions are personal strongholds introduced in the 2024 DMG. Characters gain a Bastion at 5th level, expanding it with special facilities that provide downtime benefits and passive campaign advantages.
body:
Bastions are the 2024 DMG's structured system for characters to own and develop a personal stronghold or base of operations.

**Acquiring a Bastion**
Characters gain or establish a Bastion as a campaign feature, often around 5th level. A Bastion might be a tower, guild hall, lair, temple, ship, or other fitting structure.

**Bastion Turns**
Between adventures, characters take Bastion Turns, issue orders to hirelings or defenders, and use facilities for benefits.

**Bastion Facilities**
Special facilities define what the Bastion can do and grow as the character gains levels. Facility examples include Arcane Study, Armory, Barracks, Greenhouse, Library, Pub, Sanctuary, Smithy, Stable, Trophy Room, and War Room.

The full Bastion rules are in the 2024 DMG; this is a summary reference.
tableUse: Introduce Bastions at 5th level if you want to give players a home base with mechanical weight. It works best with meaningful downtime.
commonCalls: Bastions are optional and need DM integration.
Players who love world-building and downtime will enjoy Bastions.
You decide whether enemies threaten a Bastion.
Bastions reward long-term play by making the home base valuable.
changeSummary: The 2024 DMG's Bastion system is a developed mechanical stronghold framework. The 2014 DMG discussed strongholds more narratively.
dmImpact: If running 2024 rules, Bastions give players long-term goals and make the world respond to their presence. If running 2014, see strongholds_legacy_both.
dmSummary: Bastions are personal strongholds introduced in the 2024 DMG. Characters gain a Bastion at 5th level, expanding it with special facilities that provide downtime benefits and passive campaign advantages.

Between adventures, characters take Bastion Turns, issue orders to hirelings or defenders, and use facilities for benefits.

**Bastion Facilities**
Special facilities define what the Bastion can do and grow as the character gains levels. Facility examples include Arcane Study, Armory, Barracks, Greenhouse, Library, Pub, Sanctuary, Smithy, Stable, Trophy Room, and War Room.

The full Bastion rules are in the 2024 DMG; this is a summary reference.

title: Strongholds and Headquarters
summary: High-level characters may establish strongholds - keeps, towers, temples, or guildhalls - that attract followers, generate income, and serve as campaign anchors.
body:
In the 2014 rules, strongholds are a narrative and campaign milestone rather than a fully structured mechanical system.

**When Characters Establish Strongholds**
Characters around high tier play are powerful enough to establish themselves as significant figures. They may attract followers, inspire local loyalty, and claim or construct a stronghold.

**Types of Strongholds**
| Class Type | Stronghold Type |
|-----------|----------------|
| Fighter, Paladin, Barbarian | Castle, Keep, War Camp |
| Wizard, Sorcerer | Tower, Library, Sanctum |
| Cleric, Druid | Temple, Grove, Shrine |
| Rogue, Bard | Guildhall, Inn, Theater |
| Ranger | Wilderness Outpost, Hunting Lodge |

**Stronghold Costs**
Costs and maintenance vary by structure. Use them as gold sinks and story anchors rather than pure accounting.

**2024 Note**
The 2024 DMG replaces this narrative guidance with the more mechanized Bastion system.
tableUse: Use when the party reaches high levels and wants to establish a permanent presence in the world.
commonCalls: Followers are a source of story hooks.
Stronghold attacks can create urgent side adventures.
Let characters use their stronghold meaningfully.
A stronghold should make the world acknowledge the party's power.
changeSummary: The 2014 approach is narrative and freeform. The 2024 Bastion system is mechanized with facilities, turns, and structured benefits.
dmImpact: High-level characters without a home base can feel rootless. A stronghold transforms their relationship with the world.
dmSummary: High-level characters may establish strongholds - keeps, towers, temples, or guildhalls - that attract followers, generate income, and serve as campaign anchors.

title: DC Difficulty Table
summary: Use DC 5, 10, 15, 20, 25, and 30 as the standard ladder from very easy to nearly impossible.
body: A simple ladder keeps rulings fast: 5 very easy, 10 easy, 15 medium, 20 hard, 25 very hard, and 30 nearly impossible. Adjust for leverage, tools, time pressure, and consequences.
tableUse: Pick a DC before the roll when possible, and tell players the stakes if the outcome is risky.
commonCalls: Advantage is often cleaner than lowering a DC.
Do not call for a roll when success or failure would be boring.
srdSection: SRD 5.2 > Playing the Game > D20 Tests > Difficulty Class
srdText: Difficulty Class The Difficulty Class of an ability check represents the task’s difficulty. The more difficult the task, the higher its DC. The rules provide DCs for certain checks, but the GM ultimately sets them. The Typical Difficulty Classes table presents a range of possible DCs for ability checks. Typical Difficulty Classes Task Difficulty DC Very easy 5 Easy 10 Medium 15 Hard 20 Task Difficulty DC Very hard 25 Nearly impossible 30
